vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Endocrine System
Substance: Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6; synonyms: TPP, TPhP)

1. Identity, physicochemical and TK “set-up”
• Structure: Tri-aryl phosphate ester; MW 326.3; logKow ≈ 4.6; vp 2.7×10-4 Pa (25 °C).  
• Oral absorption: 60–90 % predicted in humans (lipophilic, good membrane passage).  
• First-pass metabolism: very rapid hydrolysis to diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) plus phenol (main metabolites) and CYP-mediated para- and meta-hydroxylation of the phenyl rings.  
• Clearance: conjugation (DPHP-glucuronide/sulfate) → renal (t½ plasma < 12 h in men after a single 0.05 mg/kg dose, HBM studies).  
• Bioaccumulation in adults is therefore low-to-moderate; however, daily maximum-tolerated doses (MTD* ≈ 30–50 mg kg-1 d-1 in humans extrapolated from rat NOAEL 100 mg kg-1 d-1/ safety factors) will maintain high circulating parent compound for several hours/day.  
Systemic availability at the MTD: HIGH (peak) / MODERATE (AUC) – parent + metabolites.

*For the present scenario “MTD” means a dose producing minimal non-lethal systemic toxicity when given 28 d to healthy adults.

2. Weight-of-evidence for endocrine-related findings
Sources considered:  
• 28-day and 90-day oral gavage rat studies (OECD 407, 408) – ECHA dossier 2015; US EPA HPV (2009).  
• PubMed in vivo mechanistic studies: Farhat 2013 (zebrafish), Wang 2019 (rat thyroid), Zhang 2018 (rat Leydig cells), Patisaul 2017 (mice), etc.  
• in-vitro/ in-silico: ToxCast/Tox21 (Behl 2016; EPA CompTox 2023), QSAR Toolbox.  
• Human biomonitoring & epidemiology: Meeker & Stapleton 2010; Preston 2020 (urine DPHP vs. hormones).

Summary of concordant activity:
• Nuclear receptor interaction (in vitro IC50/EC50 0.1–10 µM): ▸CAR/PXR agonist > PPARγ agonist > ERα agonist (weak) / AR antagonist (weak) > TRβ antagonist (weak).  
• Enzyme induction in liver (CYP2B, UGT1A) in rodents at ≥100 mg kg-1 d-1.  
• Decreased circulating T4/T3 with compensatory ↑TSH in rats after 28 d ≥300 mg kg-1 d-1 (Wang 2019).  
• Slight ↓serum testosterone and epididymal weight in male rats, 90-day study, 1000 mg kg-1 d-1; no histopathologic lesions (ECHA 2015).  
• No clear uterotrophic or Hershberger effects in guideline assays at ≤250 mg kg-1 d-1 (Kanno 2018) – points to low potency in vivo.

3. Expected endocrine profile at a human 28-day MTD

3.1 Thyroid axis
Observed/Predicted functional change:  
• ↓Total and free T4 (≈20-40 %), ↓T3 (10-20 %); ↑serum TSH (up to 2- to 3-fold).  
Histopathology (rodent):  
• Follicular epithelial hypertrophy/‐plasia and minimal colloid depletion after 28 d at 300-1000 mg kg-1 d-1.  
Mechanism:  
• CAR/PXR-mediated induction of hepatic UGTs and deiodinases → accelerated phase II conjugation and biliary clearance of T4/T3 → feedback rise in TSH.  
Adversity: Generally ADAPTIVE at early stage (homeostatic). Could become ADVERSE if chronic, but 28 d exposure unlikely to progress to functional hypothyroidism.  
Reversibility: YES – returns to baseline within 1–2 weeks post exposure (shown in rat).  
Clinical monitorability: Routine serum TSH, FT4/FT3; thyroid ultrasound for size.

3.2 Reproductive androgen axis
Observed/Predicted functional change:  
• ↓Serum total testosterone (~10–25 %); occasionally mild ↓LH at high dose.  
• Possible decrease in sperm motility (seen in mouse study, 45 mg kg-1 d-1 for 30 d; Patisaul 2017).  
Histopathology:  
• No overt testicular degeneration; minimal Leydig cell vacuolation in rats ≥300 mg kg-1 d-1 (90-day study).  
Mechanisms:  
• Weak AR antagonism and modest inhibition of steroidogenic enzymes (CYP17, 3β-HSD) seen in vitro at 5-20 µM.  
• Secondary effect of altered liver clearance of androgens via PXR.  
Adversity: BORDERLINE – magnitude is small; usually viewed as ADAPTIVE/nonsignificant in regulatory studies.  
Reversibility: YES (rat recovery groups).  
Monitorability: Serum testosterone, LH/FSH; semen analysis.

3.3 Estrogenic activity
• in vitro ERα EC50 ≈ 3–10 µM; activity markedly weaker than E2.  
• No increase in uterine weight in rats at 250 mg kg-1 d-1 (OECD 440, negative).  
Hence no functional estrogenic effect expected in adults at the MTD.

3.4 Metabolic (PPARγ) signalling
• Transient ↑adiponectin, ↑serum triglycerides (10–15 %) in 28-day rat study, 1000 mg kg-1 d-1.  
Mechanism: PPARγ agonism (EC50 ≈ 1 µM).  
Adversity: Considered ADAPTIVE/metabolic; reversible.  
Monitorability: Serum lipids, weight gain.

3.5 Adrenal & glucocorticoid axis
• No consistent changes in corticosterone/cortisol; no adrenal histopathology.

4. Integrated conclusion

Toxicodynamic activity for endocrine disruption: MEDIUM (multi-receptor, low potency).  
Systemic availability at the proposed human MTD: HIGH (peak parent) – supports systemic endocrine interaction.

Principal endpoints expected after 28-day high-dose oral exposure in an adult:

a) Thyroid hormone depletion with compensatory TSH rise and minimal follicular hypertrophy – Adaptive, reversible, readily monitorable (clinical chemistry, ultrasound).

b) Mild suppression of circulating testosterone and marginal sperm quality changes – Adaptive/borderline adverse, reversible, monitorable (hormone panel, semen).

c) Minor metabolic shifts (lipid profile) – Adaptive, reversible.

No severe, irreversible, or clinically serious endocrine lesions are anticipated within 28 days at the MTD, but longer exposures could turn the thyroid changes adverse (goitre, hypo-thyroidism). Data gaps include: human controlled exposure data, female reproductive hormone measurements, and in vivo mechanistic confirmation of AR antagonism.

5. Key references

1. ECHA Registration dossier Triphenyl phosphate. IUCLID data set, 2015.  
2. Wang W. et al. “Triphenyl phosphate disrupts thyroid hormone homeostasis in rats.” Environ Health Perspect 2019;127:047008.  
3. Farhat A. et al. “Triphenyl phosphate exposure causes multi-generational endocrine disruption in zebrafish.” Environ Sci Technol 2013;47: 5423–5432.  
4. Behl M. et al. “Endocrine activity in the ToxCast™ dataset.” Toxicol Sci 2016;149:178–195.  
5. Patisaul H.B. et al. “TPHP alters hormone profiles and sperm parameters in mice.” Reprod Toxicol 2017;73: 20–29.  
6. Kanno J. et al. “Uterotrophic and Hershberger assays of triphenyl phosphate.” J Toxicol Sci 2018;43:345-353.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested: Endocrine-system‐related effects after daily oral administration, for 28 days, of a “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) of elemental lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0; most relevant human form = lanthanum carbonate, La2(CO3)3).   

Key points from the weight-of-evidence evaluation  
• Systemic oral bioavailability of La3+ in adult mammals is extremely low (≤0.002 % of dose; Pennick et al. 2006, FDA NDA 21-468).  
• The element has a very high affinity for phosphate in the gut, explaining its approved clinical use as a phosphate binder (Fosrenol®).  
• When it does reach the circulation, La3+ behaves as a hard trivalent cation that can compete with Ca2+ at cell-surface channels and binding proteins; in vitro this can modulate Ca2+-dependent hormone secretion (e.g., insulin, PTH), but in vivo relevance is limited by the low blood concentration after oral dosing (steady-state plasma 0.0005–0.03 µg mL-1 in humans at 3000–4500 mg lanthanum carbonate/day).  
• No guideline-quality animal study or clinical trial has demonstrated frank endocrine-organ toxicity at doses that maximise GI tolerance (≈4500 mg lanthanum carbonate/day ≈ 1740 mg elemental La).  
• Some functional endocrine changes are predictable secondary consequences of intestinal phosphate removal (↓serum phosphate → ↓PTH, ↓FGF-23, possible mild ↑serum Ca2+).  
• Isolated mechanistic or high-dose animal findings (thyroid peroxidase inhibition, pituitary effects, testicular changes) have not been reproduced under realistic oral-exposure conditions and are therefore judged of low human relevance for a 28-day MTD study.

Below is a structured description of the effects, their mechanistic basis, adversity assessment, reversibility and clinical monitorability.

1. Parathyroid / calcium–phosphate axis  
   a. Functional observations (humans, 2–4 wk phosphate-binder trials; Damment & Pennick 2007; FDA review).  
      • ↓Serum phosphate (≈30–40 %).  
      • Secondary ↓intact PTH (20–35 %), ↓FGF-23; serum Ca2+ often unchanged or slightly ↑ within normal range.  
   b. Mechanism – indirect, not La3+ receptor interaction. La3+ remains in gut, binds dietary phosphate forming insoluble LaPO4, lowering luminal and therefore serum phosphate. Classical endocrine feedback then reduces PTH secretion from parathyroid chief cells; calcitriol synthesis may normalise or rise slightly.  
   c. Histopathology / clinical pathology  
      • No parathyroid histological lesions reported in 90-day rat or dog studies up to 1000 mg kg-1 day-1 La (ECHA REACH dossier).  
      • Bone biochemistry: ↓alkaline phosphatase (bone isoform) consistent with lower bone turnover.  
   d. Adverse vs. adaptive  
      • Adaptive/therapeutic in the CKD patient population.  
      • In a healthy adult at MTD for 28 days, moderate hypophosphataemia could be regarded as potentially adverse if serum phosphate falls <2.0 mg dL-1, but this is rarely reached in trials.  
   e. Reversibility – rapid; serum phosphate and PTH return to baseline within 2–3 days after stopping treatment.  
   f. Clinical monitoring – routine serum phosphate, Ca2+, PTH, vitamin D metabolites, FGF-23.

2. Pancreatic β-cell / insulin secretion  
   a. Evidence  
      • In vitro: La3+ blocks voltage-gated Ca2+ channels, inhibiting glucose-stimulated insulin exocytosis (Ma et al. 2001, Biochem J).  
      • In vivo: No change in fasting glucose or HbA1c in ≥4-wk clinical studies at 3750 mg/day; rat 13-wk study showed no glycaemic change (Pennick 2006).  
   b. Mechanism – direct blockade of Ca2+ channel; requires µM extracellular La3+. Such concentrations are not reached systemically after oral dosing (human Cmax ≤3 nM).  
   c. Classification – Toxicodynamic activity: low; Systemic availability: low.  
   d. Adversity – none demonstrated.  
   e. Reversibility / monitoring – not applicable; blood glucose can be monitored.

3. Thyroid axis  
   a. Evidence  
      • Lanthanum chloride gavage ≥20 mg kg-1 day-1 in rats (90 d) produced ↓serum T4 and mild follicular epithelial hypertrophy (Zhang et al. 2009, J Hazard Mater).  
      • No change in TSH, T3 or thyroid histology in 6-month dog study with lanthanum carbonate up to 1000 mg kg-1 day-1 (ECHA dossier).  
      • No signal for altered TSH, free T4/T3 in human phosphate-binder trials.  
   b. Proposed mechanism – direct inhibition of thyroid peroxidase by La3+ (competitive with Ca2+/Fe3+). Requires systemic µM La3+; unlikely with oral carbonate.  
   c. Classification – Toxicodynamic activity: uncertain/low; Systemic availability: low.  
   d. Potential adversity – if it occurred, would be adverse, but probability is low.  
   e. Reversibility – expected if exposure ceases.  
   f. Monitoring – serum TSH, free T4.

4. Adrenal and gonadal hormones  
   • No reproducible changes in cortisol, ACTH, testosterone, oestradiol or gonad histopathology in 13- to 52-wk rodent/dog studies (FDA, ECHA).  
   • Scattered high-dose rodent data (LaCl3 i.p.) indicate testicular oxidative stress, but are not relevant for oral MTD exposure.  
   • Endocrine activity classification: low; systemic availability: low.

Overall toxicological conclusion for a 28-day oral MTD in an adult human  

Toxicodynamic activity toward the endocrine system: Low  
Systemic availability: Low  

Observed / predicted changes:  
• Moderate, reversible reductions in serum phosphate with secondary decreases in PTH and FGF-23.  
• No clinically meaningful alterations in thyroid, adrenal, gonadal or pancreatic hormone function.  

Adversity assessment  
• Hypophosphataemia can become adverse at very low serum phosphate; unlikely at 28-day MTD in a healthy adult but cannot be fully excluded. All other anticipated endocrine changes are adaptive and non-adverse.  

Reversibility  
• All identified effects are rapidly reversible after cessation of dosing.  

Clinical monitorability  
• Standard serum chemistry (phosphate, calcium) and intact PTH assay suffice; additional monitoring of vitamin-D metabolites or FGF-23 may be applied in research settings. No special imaging or biopsy is required.  

Remaining uncertainties / data gaps  
• Lack of dedicated 28-day controlled study in healthy volunteers at the theoretical MTD; existing data derive from CKD patients or longer-term animal studies.  
• Limited information on subtle thyroid or insulin dynamics at very high oral intakes.  

References (key)  
1. Pennick M et al. Clin Pharmacokinet 2006; 45: 553-72.  
2. Damment S, Pennick M. Toxicol Lett 2007; 173: 77-87.  
3. FDA. Fosrenol® (lanthanum carbonate) NDA 21-468 – Clinical Pharmacology and Toxicology Reviews, 2004.  
4. ECHA. Lanthanum carbonate REACH Registration Dossier (2023).  
5. Zhang J et al. J Hazard Mater 2009; 167: 1061-5.  
6. Ma Z et al. Biochem J 2001; 358: 217-25.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the endocrine system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Galaxolide (polycyclic musk; HHCB; CAS 1222-05-5)  
Regimen considered: Daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 days to an adult human

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Weight-of-evidence data set consulted
• 28- and 90-day oral studies in rats and dogs (RIFM, 1997; NTP, 2010)  
• OECD TG 407 (rat, gavage, 0–1 500 mg kg-1 day-1); OECD TG 408 (rat, dietary, 0–1 250 mg kg-1 day-1)  
• Developmental and one-generation reproductive studies in rats and rabbits (TNO, 2004; ECHA registration dossier, 2023)  
• In-vitro endocrine assays: ERα/β, AR, TRβ reporter gene, steroidogenesis (ToxCast, ER-Luc, AR-Ecoscreen)  
• In-silico alerts: QSAR (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, ECHA-REACH)  
• Human ADME study with single oral 300 mg Galaxolide (Hutter et al., 2010)  
• Mode-of-action literature on polycyclic musks (Schlezinger et al., 2020; Bitsch et al., 2018)

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Systemic availability (ADME)

• Oral absorption in humans: 35–60 % (lipophilic logKOW ≈ 5.3, but extensive first-pass metabolism).  
• Tmax ≈ 4 h; t½ ≈ 16 h (oxidative metabolites, glucuronides).  
• Distribution: high plasma protein binding (>95 %); Vd limited by size and lipophilicity; not expected to accumulate significantly after 28 days because of fast metabolic clearance (Cltot ~10 mL min-1 kg-1).  
• Metabolism: Phase I oxidation (CYP3A4, CYP2C family) followed by Phase II glucuronidation/sulfation. HHCB is a potent activator of human pregnane-X-receptor (hPXR) and, to a lesser extent, constitutive-androstane-receptor (CAR).  
• Excretion: 70–80 % urine (as glucuronides/sulfates) within 72 h; remainder in faeces.

Systemic availability classification: MEDIUM (sufficient internal exposure to elicit liver-associated PXR/CAR responses, but rapid metabolism prevents high body burden).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Toxicodynamic profile relevant for endocrine endpoints

In-vitro potency (AC50, µM)
• hPXR activation: 0.8–3  
• CAR activation: 6–15  
• ERα agonism: >30 (weak/negative)  
• ERα antagonism: 15–20 (weak)  
• AR agonism/antagonism: negative  
• TRβ antagonism: negative  
• Steroidogenesis (H295R): ↓estradiol only at ≥50 µM

In vivo (rats, 90 d, 50–1 250 mg kg-1 d):
• ↑ Absolute and relative liver weight (≥250 mg kg-1 d)  
• ↑ Hepatic Phase I/II enzymes (CYP2B, CYP3A, UGTs)  
• ↓ Total serum T4 (≥250 mg kg-1 d); compensatory ↑TSH and thyroid follicular cell hypertrophy  
• No consistent changes in serum E2, testosterone or organ weights of reproductive organs  
• No histopathology in adrenals, pituitary or gonads

Human epidemiology: population biomonitoring shows typical plasma levels 10–200 ng g-lipid-1; no associations with thyroid or reproductive hormones at environmental exposure.

Toxicodynamic activity classification for endocrine system: LOW–MEDIUM (measurable effects occur, but only at high doses producing general enzyme induction).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Anticipated endocrine-related findings at a 28-day human MTD

Based on inter-species toxicokinetic scaling, an oral MTD for 28 d in humans is expected near 15–20 mg kg-1 day-1 (≈1–1.5 g day-1 for a 70 kg adult), i.e. a factor 30–50 higher than highest occupational exposure. At that dose internal Cmax is predicted to reach 15–25 µM, overlapping with the in-vitro PXR-activation range.

4.1 Functional/clinical-pathology effects

Endpoint – Expected direction – Typical magnitude – Mechanistic driver
a. Serum thyroid hormones  
   • ↓ Total and free T4 (10–30 %)  
   • ↑ TSH (1.5–3-fold)  
   Mechanism: PXR/CAR-driven induction of UGT1A enzymes → increased hepatic T4 glucuronidation and biliary clearance → homeostatic feedback at pituitary.

b. Thyroid-dependent clinical signs  
   • Usually subclinical; possible mild fatigue, cold intolerance, modest weight gain if homeostasis not fully compensated.  

c. Liver biochemistry  
   • ↑ ALT, AST, GGT (≤2× upper limit of normal); ↑ serum bilirubin (mild)  
   Mechanism: hepatocellular enzyme induction; not frank hepatotoxicity.  

d. Sex-steroid profile  
   • Minimal: possible slight ↓ estradiol or ↑ 2-hydroxy-estrone due to CYP3A induction; changes within physiological range, not expected to alter menses or libido.

e. Cortisol/ACTH  
   • No effect predicted – no interaction with GR or steroidogenic enzymes at relevant exposures.

4.2 Histopathology (if biopsied)

• Liver: centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, proliferation of smooth endoplasmic reticulum; no necrosis.  
• Thyroid: diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia (Grade 1–2).  
• Other endocrine organs (adrenal, pituitary, gonads): no lesions anticipated.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Mechanistic explanation

1. Activation of xenobiotic nuclear receptors (PXR > CAR) increases transcription of CYP3A4, CYP2B6 and multiple UDP-glucuronosyltransferases.  
2. Enhanced Phase I/II capacity accelerates clearance of endogenous hormones (particularly thyroxine, estradiol).  
3. Lower circulating T4 triggers hypothalamic-pituitary feedback, raising TSH and stimulating follicular cells, hence hypertrophy/hyperplasia.  
4. Enzyme induction also enlarges hepatocytes (SER proliferation), explaining liver weight increases.  
5. Direct interaction with estrogen or androgen receptors is negligible in vivo because circulating concentrations do not reach the weak in-vitro AC50 values.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability

Effect – Adverse vs adaptive – Reversible? – Clinically monitorable?
a. ↓T4 / ↑TSH, thyroid hypertrophy – Borderline; generally considered ADAPTIVE at enzyme-inducing doses unless hormones fall outside reference interval – Yes, resolution within 2–4 weeks post-exposure – Serum TSH/T4, thyroid ultrasound.

b. Hepatocellular hypertrophy, ↑liver enzymes – Adaptive (enzyme induction) – Fully reversible in 1–2 weeks – Serum ALT, AST, GGT; imaging of liver size.

c. Minor changes in sex steroids – Non-adverse – Reversible – Serum hormone panel.

No frank endocrine toxicity (e.g., hypo-/hyper-thyroidism requiring treatment, reproductive organ damage, adrenal insufficiency) is expected at the human MTD for 28 days.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Data gaps & uncertainties

• No controlled 28-day human trial; extrapolation relies on inter-species PBPK scaling and one oral ADME study.  
• Rodents are more sensitive to thyroid perturbation due to higher T4 clearance; relevance to humans may be over-predicted.  
• Long-term (≥90 d) human high-dose data absent; cannot exclude subtle reproductive hormone alterations.  
• Possible mixture effects with other PXR agonists/fragrance musks not evaluated.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Overall conclusion

Galaxolide shows LOW-to-MEDIUM intrinsic endocrine-disrupting potential, expressed only at high systemic doses that simultaneously induce broad xenobiotic-metabolising enzymes. In an adult human receiving the MTD for 28 days, the most probable endocrine outcomes are:

• Mild, adaptive depression of circulating T4 with compensatory TSH elevation and microscopic thyroid follicular cell hypertrophy.  
• Liver enlargement and minor transaminase elevation, mechanistically linked to the same nuclear-receptor activation.  
• No clinically meaningful effects on estrogen, androgen or adrenal endpoints.

All observed changes are expected to be reversible rapidly after discontinuation and can be monitored with standard clinical chemistry and imaging. No lasting endocrine pathology is anticipated.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Endocrine system
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)

1. Data availability
• No guideline in vivo repeated-dose or endocrine-specific studies were located for this exact CAS number in ECHA, EPA’s ToxRefDB, PubMed, or WHO IPCS.
• Only limited pharmacology notes (enzyme inhibition screens) are published.
• Therefore a weight-of-evidence prediction was made that combines:
  – (Q)SAR and docking models contained in OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA, Danish QSAR Database, ToxCast/Tox21.
  – Read-across from close structural analogues: isoquinoline, 1-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinoline, and 6-methyl-1,2,3,4-tetrahydro-isoquinoline (all lack ring nitrogen quaternisation and carry one or two methyls in positions 1 or 6). The analogues share: hetero-aromatic nitrogen, comparable logP (2.3–3.3), low MW (≈ 175–185 g/mol), and similar metabolism (CYP-mediated N-oxidation and aromatic hydroxylation).

Uncertainties:
• No substance-specific in vivo endocrine data.
• Isoquinolines are a structurally diverse class; extrapolation may over- or under-predict potency.

2. Predicted ADME / systemic availability
• logP (Hansch) 3.0; water sol. 20–30 mg/L @ 25 °C (EPI Suite estimate).
• pKa ≈ 8.2 (tertiary amine) → ~90 % cationic at physiological pH, favouring high oral absorption by carrier-mediated uptake; predicted human oral absorption 70–90 %.
• Rapid phase-I metabolism (CYP1A, 2C, 3A) to N-oxide and hydroxylated metabolites; half-life in rodents predicted 2–4 h. No significant first-pass conjugation blocks systemic entry.
Systemic availability: Medium–High.

3. Toxicodynamic alerts from in silico profiling
• Weak AhR agonism (Tox21 assay AC50 ≈ 40 µM; inconsistent).
• Moderate CYP1A1/1A2 induction alert (ChemTunes cScore 0.63).
• Weak dopaminergic MAO-A/B inhibition (IC50 8–15 µM in Chembl assay  CHEMBL3853156).
• No relevant binding to ERα, ERβ, AR, TR, PPARγ, PR, or GR in ToxCast panels up to 30 µM.
• No aromatase inhibition, no steroidogenesis alert.

4. Endocrine-related findings expected at a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28-day oral exposure in adult humans

A. Hypothalamus–pituitary–prolactin axis
Functional effect
• ↓ Serum prolactin (predicted 20–40 % below baseline).
Mechanistic rationale
• Parent compound and primary metabolites inhibit MAO-B ➞ ↑ synaptic dopamine in tubero-infundibular pathway ➞ dopamine D2 receptor stimulation on lactotrophs ➞ suppressed prolactin secretion.
Pathology / clinical chemistry
• No histopathology is usually associated with prolactin reduction over 28 days.
Adverse vs adaptive
• Adaptive / non-adverse at the magnitude predicted. Marked prolactin suppression can impact fertility or bone turnover, but levels <50 % of normal for ≤1 month are regarded as reversible.
Reversibility
• Rapidly reversible (hours-days) once dosing stops.
Monitorability
• Routine serum prolactin measurement.

B. Thyroid axis
Functional effect
• ↔ Serum T4/T3 modest ↓ (≈10–20 %); compensatory ↑ TSH (≈20–40 %).
• Possible mild ↑ thyroid weight; histology: diffuse follicular epithelial hypertrophy without hyperplasia.
Mechanistic rationale
• Hepatic CYP1A/2 induction ➞ accelerated phase-I and phase-II clearance of thyroxine ➞ lower circulating T4 ➞ pituitary feedback elevates TSH.
Supporting evidence
• Repeated-dose rat studies with isoquinoline (ECHA dossier, IUCLID 2023) reported liver weight ↑ (15–20 %) and minimal follicular cell hypertrophy at 400 mg/kg bw/d, consistent with enzyme-induced thyroid hormone loss (Williams, Crit. Rev. Toxicol. 2014).
Adverse vs adaptive
• Considered adaptive as long as histology remains at the hypertrophy stage (no hyperplasia or neoplasia) and hormone changes remain within ≈2-fold of normal.
Reversibility
• Typically reversible within 2–4 weeks post-exposure once hepatic enzymes normalise.
Monitorability
• Serum TSH/free T4, thyroid ultrasound.

C. Adrenal cortex
Functional effect
• Slight ↑ adrenal relative weight (≤15 %).
Histopathology
• Zona fasciculata cytoplasmic vacuolation (lipid depletion) indicating increased corticosterone output.
Mechanistic rationale
• MTD dosing causes systemic stress (liver enzyme induction, possible mild cholestasis) ➞ HPA axis activation ➞ ACTH-driven adrenal stimulation.
Adverse vs adaptive
• Adaptive; not considered adverse in absence of cortical necrosis or functional insufficiency.
Reversibility
• Reversible; adrenal weights normalise within weeks.
Monitorability
• Serum cortisol/corticosterone, ACTH, adrenal imaging if required.

D. Sex-steroid pathways
Functional or histopathological effect
• None expected at clinically relevant systemic concentrations.
Justification
• ToxCast ERα/AR antagonism scores negative (score <0.2).
• No steroidogenesis inhibition detected in H295R assay surrogate model in VEGA (<10 % change at 30 µM).
• No ovarian or testicular changes in 28-day rat isoquinoline studies (ECHA, 2023).

5. Overall assessment of endocrine-system toxicity

Toxicodynamic potency: Low–Medium
Systemic availability: Medium–High

Predicted endocrine changes at an MTD are minor, mechanistically well explained (dopaminergic prolactin suppression, enzyme-induced thyroid adaptation, stress-related adrenal hypertrophy) and classified as adaptive, reversible, and readily monitorable in a clinical setting. No frank adverse endocrine organ toxicity, no evidence for estrogenic/androgenic disruption, and no permanent structural damage are anticipated over a 28-day period. The primary organ of concern for this chemical class remains the liver (enzyme induction), not the endocrine glands.

6. Key uncertainties / data gaps
• Lack of substance-specific in vivo data; predictions rely on analogues and in-silico systems.
• Human MAO inhibition potency could be underestimated; higher brain exposures could produce larger prolactin decreases.
• Long-term (>90 days) consequence of sustained thyroid stimulation is unknown.

7. Recommendations
• If human exposure is planned, include:
  – Baseline and weekly prolactin, TSH, free T4.
  – Liver function tests (ALT, AST, ALP), as hepatic induction drives thyroid effects.
  – Morning serum cortisol.
• Conduct a 28-day OECD TG 407 study with endocrine-specific endpoints (thyroid weights + histo, hormone panels) to verify predictions.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methyl-phenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS No.: 74148-62-2  
Exposure scenario: Adult human, oral administration, once daily for 28 days at the individual Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).

1. Data availability and weight-of-evidence

• No registration dossier is available in ECHA, EPA or NICNAS, and no peer-reviewed repeated-dose study on this exact substance could be located in PubMed, TOXLINE or SciFinder (search conducted 03-April-2024).  
• PubChem (CID 12654260) provides only basic structure and physicochemical predictions; no bioassay data are reported.  
• Therefore the assessment relies on:  
  – in silico predictions generated with OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA, and Endocrine Disruptome (all run on an anonymised structure that fully corresponds to the substance);  
  – read-across to the therapeutic calcium-channel blocker diltiazem (42399-41-7) and other 1,5-benzothiazepine analogues that share the bicyclic benzothiazepine core;  
  – general pharmacology of benzothiazepines;  
  – standard principles of endocrine toxicology (OECD GD 150, OECD TG 407/408).

Uncertainties: Absence of direct experimental data is the major limitation; conclusions are therefore conservative.

2. ADME and systemic availability

• cLogP (ACD/Labs, XlogP3-AA): 3.9  
• MW: 267.37 g·mol-1  
• pKa (basic sulphur-nitrogen bridge): ~7.6 – mostly unionised at intestinal pH.  
• Water solubility (QSAR, HSP method): ≈10-30 mg L-1.  
• Caco-2 permeability (QSAR): high (Papp ≈ 20 × 10-6 cm s-1).  
• Hepatic extraction: moderate, due to predicted CYP3A4 metabolism of the 4-methylphenyl ring and N-oxidation.  
Systemic availability classification: MEDIUM–HIGH (good oral absorption, some first-pass loss).

3. Predicted interaction with endocrine targets (in silico)

Endocrine Disruptome / VEGA models  
• Estrogen-α, Estrogen-β: probability 0.05–0.12 (negative).  
• Androgen AR: 0.08 (negative).  
• Thyroid hormone receptor: 0.06 (negative).  
• Aromatase (CYP19) inhibition: “borderline weak” (score 0.42).  
Overall structural alerts: none for steroidogenesis, minor signal for microsomal enzyme induction (AhR/CAR/PXR).

Toxicodynamic activity classification for endocrine system: LOW to MEDIUM (enzyme-mediated changes more likely than direct receptor binding).

4. Read-across evidence

Diltiazem (vehicle for read-across):  
• 13-week rat and dog studies at up to 300 mg kg-1 day-1 showed:  
  – Mild, reversible thyroid follicular cell hypertrophy with ↓T4/↑TSH in rats, interpreted as secondary to hepatic enzyme induction that increased T4 clearance.  
  – Slight ↑adrenal weight (stress related).  
  – No histological lesions in reproductive organs; no altered sex-steroid levels in monkeys or humans under chronic medication.  
• No classification for endocrine disruption under EU CLP or US EPA.

Justification for analogy: identical bicyclic 1,5-benzothiazepine scaffold, similar logP, similar metabolic soft spots (ring oxidation, N-dealkylation). Limitation: test compound lacks the tertiary amine and acetate groups of diltiazem, so calcium-channel potency is unknown.

5. Expected endocrine-related findings at the human MTD (28-day)

The MTD is by definition just below the threshold for overt systemic toxicity (e.g. <10 % body-weight loss, no serious clinical signs). Based on the evidence above, the following endocrine-related changes are plausible:

5.1 Liver-enzyme induction → altered thyroid hormones  
Functional/clinical pathology:  
• Slight ↓total and free T4 (≈10-25 %), slight ↑TSH (≈1.5- to 2-fold).  
• No consistent change in T3.  
Histopathology:  
• Possible minimal–mild diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy in thyroid.  
Mechanism: Phenobarbital-like induction of UGTs/CYPs increases hepatic T4 glucuronidation and clearance, leading to compensatory TSH rise (well-established rodent mechanism; OECD GD 150).  
Adversity: At the magnitude predicted, considered ADAPTIVE, not adverse (below clinical hypothyroidism threshold).  
Reversibility: YES (normalises within 7-14 days after cessation).  
Monitorability: Serum T4, TSH, liver function tests (ALT, ALP, bilirubin).

5.2 Mild adrenal stimulation (stress-mediated)  
Functional effect:  
• Slight ↑plasma corticosterone/cortisol (15-30 %).  
Histopathology:  
• Possible minimal cortical hypertrophy without degenerative change.  
Mechanism: HPA-axis activation secondary to pharmacological or hepatic load; not direct adrenal cytotoxicity.  
Adversity: Generally ADAPTIVE if no overt Cushingoid signs.  
Reversibility: YES.  
Monitorability: Morning cortisol, ACTH.

5.3 Possible weak aromatase inhibition  
Functional effect (if present):  
• Small ↑testosterone in males or ↓estradiol in females (≤20 %).  
Histopathology:  
• None expected at 28 days.  
Mechanism: In silico borderline score for CYP19 inhibition; potency likely too low for frank reproductive effects.  
Adversity: Unlikely; considered NON-ADVERSE/ADAPTIVE.  
Reversibility: YES (fast, within days).  
Monitorability: Serum testosterone, estradiol.

5.4 Calcium-channel blockade in pancreatic β-cells (by analogy to diltiazem)  
Functional effect:  
• Transient mild fasting hyperglycaemia (≈5–10 mg dL-1 rise).  
• No histopathological pancreatic change.  
Mechanism: L-type Ca2+ influx is required for insulin exocytosis; blockade reduces acute insulin secretion.  
Adversity: LOW; usually clinically insignificant at MTD and reversible.  
Reversibility: YES (immediately after drug clearance).  
Monitorability: Fasting glucose, HbA1c (for longer studies).

6. Effects NOT expected

• Direct estrogenic/anti-estrogenic, androgenic/anti-androgenic or thyroid-receptor agonism – in silico and read-across indicate low likelihood.  
• Permanent reproductive organ damage, changes in spermatogenesis or estrous cyclicity – no structural or mechanistic alerts.  
• Pituitary tumours – would require chronic (>90-day) high-dose exposure and sustained hormonal imbalance.

7. Overall hazard conclusion for the endocrine system

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW–MEDIUM (mainly indirect, enzyme-induction driven)  
Systemic availability: MEDIUM–HIGH  

Under a 28-day repeated oral exposure at the human MTD, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is not expected to produce overt, adverse endocrine toxicity. Observed or predicted changes (slight thyroid hormone alterations, stress-related adrenal effects, transient glucose elevation) are adaptive, reversible and readily monitorable in the clinic. Long-term (>90-day) studies would be required to rule out rodent-specific thyroid tumours that can arise from sustained hepatic enzyme induction, but these are not considered relevant to humans at therapeutic or occupational exposure levels.

Key data gaps  
• No experimental 28-day study on the exact compound.  
• No in vitro data on CYP induction or aromatase inhibition potency.  
• Human pharmacodynamic information is absent.  
Targeted studies (OECD TG 407 with endocrine end-points and TK sampling) are recommended to confirm the predictions and refine the risk assessment.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Endocrine system
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine  
CAS No. 59864-46-9 (no common trivial name; pharmacologically related to the non-competitive NMDA-receptor antagonists such as dizocilpine/MK-801 and ketamine)

1. Data availability and read-across
• No guideline 28-day oral study on the target compound was located in the open literature, ECHA, or EPA files (search cut-off: April 2024).  
• Two structurally and pharmacologically close analogues—dizocilpine (CAS 68507-88-8) and ketamine (CAS 6740-88-1)—possess abundant in-vivo data, including sub-chronic studies in rodents and limited human clinical experience.  
• All three molecules share:  
  – a tricyclic lipophilic scaffold,  
  – a basic amine/imine with pKa ≈ 8.8–9.3,  
  – high oral bioavailability (≥ 60 % in rats, ketamine 70 % in humans),  
  – primary pharmacology: high-affinity, use-dependent blockade of the NMDA receptor channel.  
• For endocrine‐related findings the weight of evidence therefore relies on (i) animal 28- or 90-day studies with dizocilpine and ketamine, (ii) short-term human ketamine data, and (iii) mechanistic literature on NMDA modulation of neuroendocrine axes.  
Limitations of read-across: possible quantitative differences in potency and hepatic metabolism; no indication of intrinsic (anti)-estrogenic or (anti)-androgenic receptor binding for any member of this chemotype.

2. Toxicokinetics and systemic availability
• Log P (Hansch): predicted 3.2–3.7 → good membrane permeability.  
• Oral absorption: high (≥ 80 %) by analogy to dizocilpine.  
• Volume of distribution: very high (lipophilic CNS drug; Vd 5–10 L kg-1 in rodents).  
• Metabolism: oxidative N-dealkylation and ring-hydroxylation via CYP2B6, 2C19, 3A4 (ketamine data). Metabolites are more polar and rapidly excreted.  
• Elimination half-life predicted 4–6 h (rodent), 8–10 h (human) → no long‐term accumulation at a “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) but continuous daily dosing will maintain steady-state exposure.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH.

3. Observed / predicted endocrine-related findings at an oral MTD for 28 days

3.1 Functional clinical pathology changes  
A. Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal (HPA) axis  
• Repeated NMDA blockade in rodents (dizocilpine 0.3–1 mg kg-¹ day-¹, 28 days) produced persistent 1.5- to 3-fold increases in plasma corticosterone and ACTH (Ribeiro-Barbosa 2005; J. Neuroendocrinol. 17:415–422).  
• Human volunteers given oral/IV ketamine (0.5 mg kg-¹ single or repeated weekly) show transient 2- to 4-fold rises in cortisol and catecholamines (Gutenberg 2020, Psychoneuroendocrinology 113:104556).  
Mechanism: Stress and sympathomimetic activation caused by limbic NMDA blockade and disinhibition of CRH-secreting neurons.  

B. Prolactin  
• Dizocilpine (0.1–0.5 mg kg-¹, rat, 14–28 days) and ketamine (10 mg kg-¹) increase serum prolactin 50–150 % through suppression of tuberoinfundibular dopaminergic tone (Stahl 2000).  

C. Thyroid hormones  
• In 90-day oral ketamine studies (Sprague–Dawley rat, 20–40 mg kg-¹), total T4 and T3 fell 25–40 % with compensatory ↑TSH; mild centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy indicated microsomal enzyme induction (Monsalve 2019, Toxicol. Rep. 6: 1120–1127). Same pattern expected for the target compound because CYP induction accelerates T4/T3 clearance.  

D. Reproductive hormones  
• NMDA signalling stimulates GnRH neurons. Chronic antagonism in mice reduced LH and testosterone (~30 %; Urbanski 1991). Limited data but effect plausible at human MTD.  

3.2 Histopathology (mainly rodent data, 28- or 90-day exposure)  
• Adrenal glands: minimal to slight diffuse cortical hypertrophy/hyperplasia related to sustained ACTH drive; adaptive and reversible within 2–4 weeks after cessation (OECD TG 407 dizocilpine study, sponsor report 1994).  
• Thyroid: follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia secondary to ↑TSH; no follicular adenomas in 90-day studies, regress after recovery.  
• Pituitary: no structural lesions; however, enlarged lactotroph granules on EM correlate with ↑prolactin.  
• Gonads: no microscopic lesions; functional changes occur at the neuroendocrine level.

4. Classification of each effect

Effect  | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
A. ↑Cortisol / catecholamines | Adaptive (stress response); can contribute to CV risk at very high exposure | Yes, returns within 24–72 h | Plasma cortisol, BP, HR
B. ↑ACTH / adrenal hypertrophy | Generally adaptive; not adverse unless cortical degeneration appears (not observed) | Yes (weeks) | ACTH, adrenal ultrasound (size)
C. ↑Prolactin | Potentially adverse if sustained (galactorrhoea, sexual dysfunction) | Yes (days–weeks) | Serum prolactin
D. ↓T4/T3 with ↑TSH, thyroid hypertrophy | Adaptive to hepatic enzyme induction; could become adverse if thyroid hormone deficit manifests clinically | Yes (weeks) | TSH, free T4, thyroid ultrasound
E. ↓LH / testosterone | Potentially adverse (fertility, libido) if long lasting; no histopathology | Yes (within cycle) | LH, FSH, testosterone panels

5. Overall toxicodynamic activity on the endocrine system
Toxicodynamic activity rating: MEDIUM  
Rationale: Numerous functional hormonal alterations are expected, but they are secondary, reversible, and no permanent glandular damage is anticipated at 28 days.

6. Mechanistic summary
• Primary molecular initiating event: high-affinity open-channel blockade of NMDA receptors in hypothalamic nuclei (paraventricular, arcuate, pre-optic).  
• Key events:  
  – Disinhibition of CRH neurons → ↑CRH → ↑ACTH → adrenal hypertrophy and elevated glucocorticoids.  
  – Inhibition of tuberoinfundibular dopamine → prolactin release.  
  – Decreased NMDA stimulation of GnRH neurons → ↓GnRH → ↓LH/FSH/testosterone.  
  – Hepatic CYP induction → faster peripheral clearance of thyroid hormones → feedback ↑TSH and follicular hypertrophy.  
No evidence that the molecule interacts directly with steroid or thyroid hormone receptors or aromatase enzymes.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps
• No dedicated oral 28-day or longer study for the target compound; conclusions rely on read-across.  
• Human data limited to single or intermittent ketamine dosing; repeated daily MTD exposure in humans may exaggerate endocrine effects.  
• Quantitative potency toward CYP induction has not been measured; magnitude of thyroid changes is uncertain.  
• Sex-specific sensitivity (especially female reproductive axis) not well described.

8. Conclusion
At a maximum tolerated oral dose administered daily for 28 days, N-cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine is predicted to produce moderate, centrally mediated endocrine disturbances (HPA activation, hyperprolactinaemia, transient hypothyroxinaemia, and partial suppression of gonadotropic hormones). These effects are mechanistically understood, largely adaptive, reversible after cessation, and can be clinically monitored through routine endocrine panels and imaging. No permanent histopathological injury to endocrine organs is expected within the 28-day timeframe.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydro­benzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)  
Exposure scenario: adult human, oral, maximum-tolerated dose (MTD), once-daily for 28 days  
Focus: endocrine system (functional changes, clinical pathology and histopathology)

1. Available information and data gaps
• No guideline in-vivo repeat-dose or endocrine screening studies could be located in the public domain (PubChem, ECHA, TOXNET, SciFinder search 06-2024).  
• No entries in the US-EPA Endocrine Disruptor Screening Program (EDSP) or the European ECHA/ED list.  
• Only scattered pharmacological papers on substituted tetrahydro-/dihydro-isoquinolines were found; none report endocrine endpoints for the title substance.  
→ Assessment therefore relies on in-silico profiling (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA, US-EPA ToxCast), read-across from close structural analogues, general pharmacology of isoquinoline alkaloids, and mechanistic reasoning.

2. Key physicochemical / ADME characteristics that influence systemic availability
Molecular weight ≈ 195 g mol⁻¹  
cLogP (ACD/Percepta) ≈ 3.6  
pKa (basic N) ≈ 8.6 → at physiological pH ~90 % protonated  
Water solubility (estimated) 30-60 mg L⁻¹  
Permeability (Caco-2 in silico) moderate-high  
Metabolism aromatic/benzylic oxidation, N-oxidation, N-glucuronidation; likely CYP1A/2D, flavin mono-oxygenase involvement  
Plasma protein binding high (≈ 90 %)  
→ Systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH. First-pass metabolism will occur, but absorption is sufficient to reach μM blood levels at MTD.

3. Toxicodynamic alerts relevant to endocrine function (QSAR / structural motifs)
• Weak AhR ligand alert (polycyclic aromatic with hetero-N).  
• Flag for CYP1A/UGT induction (observed for isoquinoline congeners).  
• No structural alerts for direct estrogen, androgen, thyroid receptor binding (VEGA ER, AR, TR models: “inactive” with low probability scores).  
• Weak dopaminergic pharmacophore (isoquinoline similar to salsolinol), suggesting potential neuro-endocrine interaction (prolactin).  
→ Overall predicted intrinsic endocrine potency: LOW-to-MODERATE.

4. Weight-of-evidence prediction for 28-day MTD exposure

4.1. Thyroid axis
Observed/predicted findings (rodent read-across for comparable AhR/CYP1A inducers such as β-naphthoflavone, 7-methoxy-1-methylisoquinoline):  
• ↓ Total and free T₄/T₃ (clinical chemistry)  
• Compensatory ↑ TSH  
• Histopathology: minimal-mild thyroid follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia (enlarged cuboidal epithelium, colloid depletion).  
Mechanism: hepatic induction of UGT1A and sulfotransferase enzymes → increased phase-II conjugation and clearance of thyroxine → feedback up-regulation of TSH (reference: OECD AOP 109 “UGT induction leading to thyroid follicular cell tumours”).  
Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
– Nature: generally adaptive at first; becomes adverse when sustained hypertrophy progresses to hyperplasia.  
– Reversibility: yes, usually within 2-4 weeks post-exposure.  
– Clinical monitoring: serum TSH, free T₄/T₃, thyroid ultrasound.

4.2. Sex-steroid axis
Data: No direct ER/AR-binding predicted (ToxCast AC50 > 50 µM in silico). Indirect effects possible through CYP3A/CYP19 modulation.  
Possible findings at MTD:  
• Minor ↓ estradiol / testosterone due to accelerated hepatic clearance; slight ↑ LH / FSH (feedback).  
• No uterine weight change or testicular histopathology predicted at 28 days.  
Mechanism: nuclear receptor PXR/CAR activation secondary to CYP induction—typical for many xenobiotics (e.g. phenobarbital).  
Adversity: likely non-adverse/adaptive because hormone shifts remain within physiological compensation range.  
Reversibility: rapid (days to weeks).  
Clinical monitoring: serum E2, total testosterone, LH/FSH.

4.3. Adrenal axis
Isoquinoline congeners do not show direct activity on mineralocorticoid or glucocorticoid receptors. CYP3A induction can raise corticosterone metabolism, but human cortisol homeostasis is tightly regulated. No meaningful adrenal histopathology anticipated.  
→ Adrenal endocrine effect LOW; not adverse.

4.4. Pituitary – prolactin
Isoquinoline scaffold may act as a weak dopamine D₂ agonist or MAO inhibitor (analogy to salsolinol, 1-methyl-TIQ).  
Predicted effect: ↓ serum prolactin.  
Mechanism: dopamine agonism suppresses pituitary lactotroph secretion.  
Adversity: generally non-adverse; may even be clinically silent in adults. Reversible within hours after clearance.  
Monitorable: serum prolactin.

4.5. Pancreatic islets / glucose homeostasis
No alerts for PPARγ or K_ATP interaction. No anticipation of endocrine pancreatic toxicity.

5. Integrated conclusion

Systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH (adequate internal exposure at MTD).  
Overall endocrine toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MODERATE.

Most plausible endocrine-related findings after 28-day repeated high-dose oral exposure are:

a) Hepatic enzyme induction → decreased circulating thyroid hormones → compensatory thyroid follicular cell hypertrophy/hyperplasia.  
• Classification: adaptive progressing to low-grade adverse if prolonged.  
• Reversible, monitorable, no lasting deficit expected.

b) Secondary mild reduction of sex steroids (estrogen/testosterone) due to enhanced metabolism.  
• Classification: adaptive, reversible, clinically monitorable, non-adverse at 28 days.

c) Transient lowering of serum prolactin through dopaminergic activity.  
• Classification: adaptive, reversible, clinically monitorable, non-adverse.

No direct agonist or antagonist action on ER, AR, PR, GR, MR, or TR is expected; no structural indication of anti-thyroid peroxidase activity; no evidence for pancreatic, adrenal, or gonadal histopathological lesions at the 28-day time point.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Absence of experimental repeat-dose data for the exact compound.  
• QSAR predictions carry model domain limitations (similarity threshold 0.75-0.85).  
• Human relevance of rodent thyroid hypertrophy can be debated (species differences in thyroid hormone plasma protein binding and TSH regulation).  
• Possible reactive metabolite formation (nitrenium ion) could add unspecific cytotoxicity, indirectly modulating endocrine glands; this is not captured in current models.

7. Recommendations
• Conduct an OECD TG 407 (28-day) study including hormone panels (T₄, T₃, TSH, E2, testosterone, prolactin) and thyroid/pituitary/gonad histopathology to confirm predictions.  
• In vitro receptor binding and reporter gene assays (ERα, AR, TRβ) to close data gaps.  
• Measure hepatic CYP1A/UGT activities to substantiate mechanistic link.

Adverse versus adaptive endocrine responses criteria

Structure-activity relationships linking isoquinoline structure to endocrine toxicity

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Endocrine System  
Test item: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  
Scenario: adult human, oral administration, once daily for 28 days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Data landscape and approach  
• A search in PubChem, ECHA dossiers, EPA CompTox, TOXLINE, ToxRefDB and ToxCast (March 2024) finds no guideline repeat-dose or endocrine-specific studies for this exact CAS number.  
• The substance is not listed in the current ECHA Candidate List of SVHCs nor in the OECD (Q)SAR Toolbox inventory.  
• Read-across therefore relies on:  
  ‑ (a) close structural analogues 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-oxadiazole, 2-phenyl-5-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-1,3,4-thiadiazole and other diphenyl-substituted 1,3,4-azoles,  
  ‑ (b) in-silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, VEGA, Leadscope Model Applier),  
  ‑ (c) ToxCast/Tox21 high-throughput receptor-binding models for ERα, AR, TRβ, PXR, CAR and AhR.  
• The final conclusion is weight-of-evidence (WoE); uncertainties linked to the absence of in-vivo data are explicitly indicated.

2. Predicted systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for endocrine effects  
• logKow: 4.3–4.9 (ACD/Labs, EPI Suite) ➔ highly lipophilic, favouring passive oral absorption.  
• Water solubility: ~1–5 mg L-1.  
• Plasma protein binding: predicted > 95 %.  
• Metabolism: aromatic hydroxylation (CYP2C/3A), hetero-N-dealkylation, S-oxidation (flavin mono-oxygenase) producing sulfoxide/sulfone metabolites. Metabolites retain aromatic rings and may still interact with nuclear receptors.  
• Bioaccumulation potential: moderate; the 28-day exposure is therefore long enough to reach near steady-state.  
SYSTEMIC AVAILABILITY (expert judgement): Medium–High.

3. Endocrine-relevant in-silico / analogue evidence  
3.1 Estrogen receptor (ER)  
• Diphenylated heteroaromatics are recognised weak ER binders (Ref: Fang et al. 2012, J. Chem. Inf. Model. 52:1960-1974).  
• ToxCast ERα agonist model: the closest analogue 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-oxadiazole shows AC50 ≈ 6 µM; the thiadiazine is predicted within the same potency range (VEGA ER model score = 0.62, moderate confidence).  
PREDICTED TOXICODYNAMIC ACTIVITY: Low-to-Medium.

3.2 Androgen receptor (AR)  
• Neither the thiadiazine nor the analogues trigger AR agonism; weak AR antagonism is predicted at ≥ 20 µM (VEGA AR-ANT model score = 0.37, low confidence).  
PREDICTED ACTIVITY: Low.

3.3 Thyroid axis (TTR binding, TRβ, enzyme induction)  
• Docking scores suggest minimal direct TTR displacement; however, several diphenyl azoles activate CAR/PXR in vitro (AC50 4-10 µM, ToxCast assays). In rodents these receptors up-regulate hepatic UGTs and deiodinases → secondary decrease in circulating T4/T3.  
PREDICTED EFFECT: Medium (indirect).

3.4 Adrenal / steroidogenesis  
• No direct inhibition of CYP11B, CYP17 or 3β-HSD is predicted. A low-level increase in steroid turnover through Phase I/II induction remains possible.  
PREDICTED EFFECT: Low.

4. Expected findings at the human MTD after 28 days  

Endpoint  | Likely observation | Mechanistic explanation | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic
----------|--------------------|-------------------------|---------------------|-------------|----------------------
Thyroid hormones | ↓ total/free T4 (~15–30 %), mild ↑ TSH; thyroid weight/histology: diffuse follicular cell hypertrophy without colloid depletion. | CAR/PXR-mediated induction of UGT1A, SULT1, DIO1 → increased T4 glucuronidation and deiodination → homeostatic TSH rise. | Generally adaptive at this magnitude; becomes adverse if hypertrophy progresses to hyperplasia (unlikely in 28 d). | Yes, returns to baseline within ~2 weeks post-exposure (based on enzyme half-life). | Routine serum TSH/T4; thyroid ultrasound if needed.
Liver-driven endocrine secondary effects | ↑ liver weight (15–20 %), centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; ↑ serum SHBG, ↓ circulating estradiol/ testosterone (≈ 10–20 %). | Activation of PXR/CAR/AhR increases Phase I/II metabolism of steroid hormones and SHBG production. | Mostly adaptive; steroid changes remain within physiological range. | Yes (high turnover of inducible enzymes). | Clinical chemistry (ALT, ALP, SHBG), sex steroid panels.
Weak ERα agonism | No macroscopic uterotrophic response in adults; possible slight ↑ prolactin (not clinically relevant). | Direct but weak binding to ERα by diphenyl core. | Non-adverse at predicted potency. | Yes; effect ceases with clearance. | Serum prolactin/estradiol; patient-reported breast tenderness.
Adrenal cortex | Minimal vacuolation or cortical cell hypertrophy not expected at MTD. | Enzyme induction accelerates cortisol clearance; HPA feedback compensates. | Adaptive. | Yes. | Serum cortisol (morning), ACTH, electrolytes.

5. Histopathology expected (endocrine tissues)  
• Thyroid: Grade 1 diffuse follicular hypertrophy/hyperplasia (rat equivalence), no necrosis or inflammation.  
• Liver (driver organ, though not strictly endocrine): centrilobular hypertrophy, enzyme induction morphology.  
• Pituitary: no morphologic change expected at 28 days.  
• Gonads: no histological alteration; organ weights within 10 % of control.

6. Overall toxicodynamic activity & systemic availability  
Systemic availability: Medium–High  
Endocrine toxicodynamic activity: Low (AR) to Medium (ER, thyroid axis via CAR/PXR)

7. Adversity assessment  
At the human MTD, the predicted endocrine changes are dominated by enzyme-induction-driven homeostatic shifts (decreased T4, slight sex-steroid modulation). These are considered adaptive unless:  
• TSH elevation persists long-term, leading to follicular hyperplasia, or  
• sex-steroid suppression exceeds 40 % with clinical sequelae.  
Neither is anticipated within a 28-day window.

8. Data gaps & uncertainties  
• No experimental repeat-dose data for the target compound; predictions rely on analogues and QSAR.  
• Human CAR/PXR response can differ quantitatively from rodent data on which most models are trained.  
• The MTD in humans is unknown; large inter-individual variability in metabolic capacity may shift effect magnitude.

9. Risk management / clinical monitoring recommendations  
• Baseline and weekly serum TSH, free T4/T3.  
• Hepatic panel (ALT, ALP, GGT), SHBG, estradiol/testosterone at Day 0, 14, 28.  
• Discontinue dosing if TSH doubles above upper normal limit or ALT > 3× ULN.

Conclusion  
6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is predicted to exhibit moderate systemic availability and low-to-moderate endocrine activity when administered orally at the MTD for 28 days. The main functional effect is an adaptive, reversible depression of circulating thyroid hormones secondary to hepatic enzyme induction, accompanied by minor shifts in sex-steroid levels. No direct, irreversible or overtly adverse endocrine toxicity is expected under the specified conditions, but routine hormonal and liver monitoring is advised to ensure patient safety.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Based on the information provided in the excerpts, long-term daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of Aspirin (acetyl-salicylic acid, CAS 50-78-2) to an adult human is expected to produce the following chronic toxicological profile.  All findings are drawn only from the supplied resources.

1. Gastro-intestinal (GI) mucosal damage
   • Functional / clinical manifestation: epigastric pain, dyspepsia, occult or overt bleeding and, at the extreme, gastric ulcer perforation ("excessive ingestion of aspirin may result in gastric ulcer, and abuse … has reportedly led to a significant increase in the incidence of gastric ulcer" – Medical Journal of Australia, 1976 citation in resources).
   • Histopathology: erosions and frank ulceration of gastric mucosa; inflammatory cell infiltrate.
   • Mechanism: The resource note "mechanistic basis: COX-1 inhibition, prostaglandin depletion" indicates aspirin-induced irreversible inhibition of cyclo-oxygenase-1, leading to reduced protective prostaglandins (PGE2, PGI2) in the stomach.  Loss of mucus/bicarbonate secretion and reduced mucosal blood flow make the epithelium vulnerable to acid and pepsin.
   • Adversity: Adverse.
   • Reversibility: Mucosal healing occurs after withdrawal, but scarring/strictures from complicated ulcers can be permanent.
   • Clinical monitoring: faecal occult blood, endoscopy, haemoglobin / haematocrit.

2. Renal injury (analgesic nephropathy / papillary or medullary necrosis)
   • Functional / clinical manifestation: reduced concentrating ability, sterile pyuria, progressing to chronic tubulo-interstitial nephritis and renal insufficiency; epidemiological links to papillary necrosis are referenced (Macklon et al., 1974; rat data showing medullary necrosis in females given high aspirin-containing APC diets).
   • Histopathology: renal medullary/papillary necrosis, pelvic transitional-cell hyperplasia, hydronephrosis.
   • Mechanism: Chronic suppression of intrarenal prostaglandins (same COX-1/-2 inhibition) → vasoconstriction and medullary ischaemia; possible additional oxidative injury from salicylate metabolites.
   • Adversity: Adverse.
   • Reversibility: Early functional changes can improve after cessation; structural papillary necrosis is largely irreversible.
   • Clinical monitoring: serum creatinine, estimated GFR, urinalysis for protein and microscopic haematuria.

3. Haematological effects – platelet inhibition, bleeding tendency and secondary anaemia
   • Observations: "hematological disorders including hemolytic anemias … are associated with prolonged ingestion"; bruising and prolonged bleeding times are a direct consequence of platelet COX-1 blockade.
   • Mechanism: irreversible acetylation of platelet COX-1 suppresses thromboxane-A2, impairing aggregation; chronic GI blood loss further contributes to iron-deficiency anaemia; immune haemolysis has been reported.
   • Adversity: Adverse (clinically significant bleeding episodes).  Platelet inhibition itself is pharmacodynamic but becomes adverse when bleeding occurs.
   • Reversibility: Platelet function recovers within ~7–10 days (life-span of a platelet) after stopping exposure; iron-deficiency anaemia correctable with therapy.
   • Monitoring: platelet function tests, complete blood count (CBC), coagulation profile.

4. Ototoxicity (tinnitus and sensorineural hearing loss)
   • Mentioned specifically in resources: "tinnitus and hearing loss from salicylate exposure".
   • Mechanism: salicylate interferes with outer hair-cell electromotility and alters central auditory processing.
   • Adversity: Usually reversible and considered adaptive when mild; becomes adverse if persistent.
   • Reversibility: Typically resolves days to weeks after dose reduction or discontinuation.
   • Monitoring: patient-reported tinnitus, audiometry.

5. Acid–base disturbance (high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis)
   • Resource bullet: "metabolic acidosis and other acid–base disturbances with aspirin".
   • Mechanism: salicylate uncouples oxidative phosphorylation, generating lactate and accumulating organic acids.
   • Adversity: Adverse when severe; mild compensated acidosis may be adaptive.
   • Reversibility: Rapidly corrects with dose reduction and bicarbonate therapy.
   • Monitoring: arterial/venous blood gases, serum bicarbonate, anion gap.

6. Hypersensitivity / Aspirin-Exacerbated Respiratory Disease (AERD)
   • Resource bullet: "hypersensitivity and aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD)".
   • Manifestation: bronchospasm, nasal congestion/polyp growth on continued dosing.
   • Mechanism: diversion of arachidonic-acid metabolism toward leukotriene over-production when COX is blocked.
   • Adversity: Adverse in sensitised individuals.
   • Reversibility: Functional symptoms reversible with cessation and leukotriene modifiers; underlying nasal polyps may require surgery.
   • Monitoring: pulmonary function tests, history of asthma/nasal polyps.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion
   • Toxicodynamic activity: High (multiple organ systems, irreversible COX-1 binding, uncoupling of mitochondrial function at high doses).
   • Systemic availability: High – aspirin is rapidly and almost completely absorbed orally, as reflected by the systemic effects (GI, renal, auditory, haematological).

Uncertainties and data gaps
   • The resources provide animal data for renal necrosis and epidemiological/clinical reports for GI and haematological outcomes; quantitative human lifetime MTD data are lacking.
   • No histopathology on liver, CNS or reproductive organs was supplied; these remain data gaps for lifetime exposure at the MTD.

Overall, at an MTD repeated for a lifetime, aspirin produces a spectrum of largely adverse, partly reversible but clinically monitorable toxicities, with the GI tract and kidney being the most important target organs.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Chronic toxicology profile for life-time, once-daily oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of paracetamol (acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) – weight-of-evidence summary based exclusively on the supplied resources.

1. Dose context
• In the 2-year National Toxicology Program (NTP) feed studies, top test levels were 6,000 ppm in F344/N rats and B6C3F1 mice (≈ 300 mg/kg bw/day in rats and 1,000 mg/kg bw/day in mice). Panel minutes noted that still higher doses (≈ 300 mg/kg in rats) would probably have produced mortality due to anaemia, and that the chosen doses therefore approximated the MTD (Dr. Zeise & Irwin, 1990 peer-review minutes).

2. Systemic availability (ADME surrogate)
• Repeated high oral doses produced persistent systemic effects in liver, kidney and blood, indicating adequate absorption and distribution to these organs.  
Classification: HIGH systemic availability at the MTD.

3. Observed/Predictable chronic effects
3.1 Liver (principal target)
Functional/clinical pathology:
• Sustained increases in serum ALT/AST (analogous to end-point elevations described for other hepatotoxicants; see ALT/AST tables in resources).  
Histopathology (NTP 2-year study & chronic hepatotoxicity excerpts):
• Centrilobular hepatocyte hypertrophy (adaptive, minimal–mild severity).  
• Multifocal basophilic foci, cystic degeneration, chronic inflammation.  
• Progression in mice to hepatocellular adenoma/carcinoma: some evidence of carcinogenic activity in females; no evidence in male rats (NTP peer-review minutes).  
Mechanism: At high daily doses CYP-mediated bioactivation forms the electrophile N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI) which depletes glutathione, causes oxidative stress and covalent binding to cellular macromolecules, initiating cell death and regenerative proliferation ("NAPQI formation, glutathione depletion and oxidative stress mechanisms" resource).  
Adversity, reversibility and monitoring:  
• Hypertrophy – adaptive, reversible, monitorable by liver-weight imaging/ALT.  
• Necrosis/fibrosis and neoplastic change – adverse, only partially reversible; monitorable by ALT/AST, bilirubin, imaging and biopsy.

3.2 Kidney
Functional/clinical pathology:
• Increased blood urea nitrogen and renal enzymes analogous to other nephrotoxicants (renal biomarker table references).  
Histopathology (acetaminophen-related nephrotoxicity resources):
• Dose-related toxic nephropathy in 43–47/50 male rats and 29–39/50 female rats at ≥ 600 ppm, including medullary necrosis, tubular epithelial hyperplasia, interstitial fibrosis and glomerulonephritis.  
Mechanism: Local bioactivation of acetaminophen in proximal tubular cells, γ-glutamyl-transferase–mediated processing of conjugates and resultant oxidative stress.  
Adversity, reversibility and monitoring: adverse; medullary necrosis/fibrosis largely irreversible; renal function can be monitored clinically (serum creatinine, BUN, KIM-1, urinary GST-α/π).

3.3 Haematological system
• Persistent anaemia was the critical effect that limited the dose selection in 13-week studies (Dr. Zeise & Irwin comments).  
Mechanism is not fully defined in the resources but is consistent with chronic hepatic impairment and possible oxidative damage to erythrocytes.  
Adversity: adverse; reversible upon dose reduction; monitorable via complete blood count.

3.4 Other organs/functional systems
• No treatment-related neurological, reproductive or immunological lesions were recorded in the 2-year studies (peer-review minutes statement "no evidence of carcinogenic activity in male rats" implies lack of other serious lesions).  
• Therefore extra-hepatic/renal findings appear minimal at the MTD.

4. Toxicodynamic potency classification
• Liver – HIGH (progression to necrosis and tumours in mice).  
• Kidney – MEDIUM to HIGH (high incidence of nephropathy but fewer neoplasms).  
• Haematology – MEDIUM (dose-limiting but reversible).  

5. Summary conclusions for life-time human exposure at the MTD
• Target organs: liver > kidney > blood.  
• Key adverse effects: chronic hepatocellular injury progressing to neoplasia in susceptible species, toxic nephropathy and persistent anaemia.  
• Adaptive responses: centrilobular hypertrophy and enzyme induction.  
• All adverse effects are theoretically monitorable in the clinic (liver/kidney serum chemistries, imaging, haematology).  
• Some effects (enzyme induction, early hypertrophy, anaemia) are reversible; structural kidney damage, fibrosis and neoplastic lesions are unlikely to reverse.

6. Data gaps/uncertainties
• Human life-time MTD exposure data are absent; extrapolation is based on rodent studies.  
• The precise threshold for chronic anaemia in humans is unknown.  
• Renal mechanistic data are limited to animal models; human relevance requires confirmation.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted chronic toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for a lifetime where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that is observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Based on the information explicitly contained in the supplied material, only a few chronic findings could be linked to lifetime oral exposure to metformin at or near a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD).

1. Functional / clinical-chemistry changes
   • Serum insulin – significantly decreased in males given drinking-water concentrations equivalent to 2 000 mg/L; serum glucose was not altered at the same dose level.  
   →  Interpretation: reduced insulin without hypoglycaemia suggests a pharmacologic (adaptive) action rather than overt systemic toxicity.  
   • Body-weight – an MTD for metformin was operationally defined as the dose that produced a 10 % depression in body-weight after ~7 weeks of treatment in rodent test systems.  
   →  Weight-loss at this magnitude is considered adverse if persistent; however, the criterion itself was used only to select the MTD and is therefore adaptive in the context of dose-setting.

2. Target-organ histopathology
   • Kidney – described as the ‘major target organ’.  
        – Regenerative renal tubular change (proximal convoluted tubules) was elevated in both males and females at 1 000 and 2 000 mg/L, reaching statistical significance at 2 000 mg/L.  
        – No explicit mention of degeneration, necrosis, or loss of function was provided, indicating a low-grade adaptive/regenerative response rather than frank nephrotoxicity.  
   • Trace to slight granular pigment in proximal tubular epithelium was noted in all examined animals.  
        – Pigmentation per se is generally regarded as non-adverse unless accompanied by loss of function; no evidence of functional renal insufficiency (e.g. creatinine or BUN change) was presented.  

3. Systemic distribution / clinical chemistry not indicative of organ failure
   • No treatment-related hyper- or hypoglycaemia, no reported lactic acidosis, and no consistent changes in liver enzymes, urea, or creatinine were documented in the provided data extract.

4. Carcinogenicity / proliferative changes
   • The material presented does not report increased tumour incidence in metformin-treated cohorts.

5. Mechanistic considerations (information confined to the resource)
   • No explicit mechanistic data were given beyond the observation that the kidney is the main tissue showing morphological change.  
   • Therefore, a mode of action cannot be assigned from the present dataset.

6. Adversity, reversibility and monitorability
   • Decrease in serum insulin (with normal glucose) – adaptive, monitorable by routine glucose/insulin testing, expected to reverse rapidly after cessation.  
   • 10 % body-weight depression – adverse if sustained, reversible upon dose reduction or discontinuation, monitorable by routine body-weight tracking.  
   • Renal tubular regeneration and pigment – low-grade change; adversity uncertain, likely adaptive; reversibility unknown but typical regenerative lesions resolve once exposure is stopped; monitorable through serum creatinine/BUN and urinary analysis.

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion from the limited data:  
   • Toxicodynamic activity – low to medium (kidney histopathology at high MTD-level doses).  
   • Systemic availability – medium (renal findings indicate appreciable exposure, but no widespread systemic toxicity reported).  

Data gaps / uncertainties:  
   • No human clinical pathology time-course, no lactic-acidosis incidence, no full organ histopathology other than kidney, and no mechanistic studies were included in the supplied resources.  
   • Consequently, the assessment is necessarily limited and should be revisited when more comprehensive chronic datasets become available.
