vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Chemical: Atorvastatin  
Synonyms: ( ± )-trans-1,2,3,7,8,8a-Hexahydro-3,7-dimethyl-8-[2-(4-fluorophenyl)-5-(1-methylethyl)-3-phenyl-4-(phenylcarbamoyl)-1H-pyrrol-1-yl]-1-naphthalenyl 2-(di-hydroxy-2-propyl)-4-fluorophenyl-2-methyl-propanoate, etc.  
CAS: 134523-00-5 (free acid; marketed clinically as the calcium salt, CAS 134523-03-8)

I.  ADME SUMMARY AND SYSTEMIC AVAILABILITY  

1. Absorption (oral): ~30 % of the administered dose is absorbed; absolute bioavailability of the parent is ≈14 % because of marked first-pass extraction in the liver (primary site of pharmacologic action).  
2. Distribution: Highly plasma-protein bound (> 98 %). Large volume of distribution (~380 L) with strong hepatic extraction mediated mainly by OATP1B1/1B3 transporters. Limited penetration across the blood–brain barrier; detectable, but low, cerebrospinal fluid concentrations.  
3. Metabolism: Extensive CYP3A4 oxidation to ortho- and para-hydroxy-atorvastatin and β-oxidation products that retain HMG-CoA-reductase inhibitory activity.  
4. Excretion: ~70 % in bile, 30 % in urine (mainly as metabolites). t½ ≈ 14 h (active inhibitory equivalents ≈ 20–30 h).  
Systemic availability to extra-hepatic tissues is therefore low-to-moderate, whereas the liver is exposed to high concentrations.

Assessment:  
• Systemic availability: MEDIUM (liver HIGH, skeletal muscle LOW-to-MEDIUM, most other organs LOW).  
• Intrinsic toxicodynamic activity: MEDIUM. Pharmacologic potency is high, but off-target toxicity is usually threshold-dependent and often adaptive below the maximum tolerated dose (MTD).

II.  CHRONIC (LIFETIME) DAILY ADMINISTRATION AT THE MTD – EXPECTED TOXICOLOGICAL PROFILE  

The “maximum tolerated dose” in humans is not formally established for atorvastatin but clinical doses of 80 mg/day are considered the highest tolerated. For the purpose of this assessment “MTD” is assumed to be 80 mg/day given orally for a lifetime.

A. Hepatobiliary effects  

1. Functional / clinical pathology  
   • Reversible elevation of serum ALT/AST (≥ 3× upper limit of normal in ≈ 1 % of treated subjects at 80 mg).  
   • Rare progression (< 1 per 100 000 patient-years) to drug-induced liver injury (DILI) or acute liver failure.  
2. Histopathology (animal data at high multiples of human AUC)  
   • Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, increased liver weight, periportal lipid depletion, minimal necrosis.  
   • Rat/mouse: increased hepatic adenomas at ≥ 400 mg/kg/day (≈ 125× human AUC).  
3. Mechanism  
   • Pharmacologic up-regulation of SREBP-controlled cholesterol synthesis genes → smooth ER proliferation → hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive).  
   • High intra-hepatocellular concentrations plus CYP3A4/β-oxidation → ROS formation, mitochondrial stress and immune idiosyncrasy (adverse DILI).  
4. Classification  
   • Mild transaminase increases: Adaptive, Reversible, Monitorable (liver function tests).  
   • Clinically significant DILI: Adverse, Potentially irreversible (if fulminant), Monitorable (ALT/AST, bilirubin, INR, Hy’s law criteria).

B. Skeletal-muscle effects  

1. Functional / clinical pathology  
   • Myalgia/myopathy (≈ 2–5 % at 80 mg, creatine kinase [CK] ≤ 10×ULN).  
   • Rare rhabdomyolysis (≈ 3–4 per 100 000 patient-years).  
2. Histopathology (humans & animals)  
   • Focal necrosis, fiber degeneration, macrophage infiltration predominantly in type II fibers.  
3. Mechanism  
   • Inhibition of mevalonate pathway → ↓ isoprenoids and Coenzyme Q10 → impaired mitochondrial respiration and Ca²⁺ handling → myocyte energy crisis and necrosis, exacerbated by high plasma levels (e.g., OATP1B1 polymorphism, CYP3A4 inhibitors).  
4. Classification  
   • Myalgia/myopathy: Adverse, Usually Reversible, Monitorable (CK, symptoms).  
   • Rhabdomyolysis: Adverse, Potentially irreversible (renal failure sequelae), Monitorable (CK, myoglobin, renal function).

C. Metabolic / endocrine effects – New-onset diabetes mellitus  

1. Functional findings  
   • ~9 % relative increase in incident type 2 diabetes (meta-analyses; NNH ≈ 250 over 4 y at high dose).  
2. Mechanism  
   • Decreased prenylation of GLUT4 regulatory G-proteins, impaired β-cell Ca²⁺ signaling, reduced adipocyte insulin sensitivity → mild hyperglycaemia.  
3. Classification  
   • Adverse (chronic disease), Irreversible once established but Clinically monitorable (fasting glucose, HbA1c). Risk:benefit remains favorable for dyslipidaemic patients.

D. Renal effects (secondary)  

1. Functional / clinical pathology  
   • Elevated serum creatinine and acute tubular necrosis secondary to myoglobinuria in rhabdomyolysis.  
2. Mechanism  
   • Myoglobin precipitation, oxidative stress in renal tubules.  
3. Classification  
   • Adverse, Reversible if managed promptly, Monitorable (serum creatinine, urine output, urinalysis).

E. Central nervous system / cognitive  

1. Functional observations  
   • Case reports of reversible memory loss, sleep disturbances; cohort data overall neutral.  
2. Mechanism  
   • Possible cholesterol depletion in neuronal membranes affecting synaptic vesicle release; reduced isoprenoid signaling.  
3. Classification  
   • Mostly Adaptive/Benign; Reversible; Difficult but Monitorable via neurocognitive tests.

F. Ophthalmic effects  

1. Animal lifetime studies (dogs, rats): increased cataract frequency at ≥ 6× human AUC; not confirmed in humans.  
2. Mechanism  
   • Osmotic or oxidative changes due to lens cholesterol reduction.  
3. Classification  
   • Potentially Adverse; Uncertain reversibility; Monitorable (slit-lamp examination). Human relevance LOW.

G. Carcinogenicity (rodent lifetime bioassays)  

1. Male mice: hepatocellular adenoma/carcinoma↑; female rats: cholangioma↑ only at very high doses (≥ 400 mg/kg/day).  
2. Mechanism  
   • Chronic hepatocellular proliferation driven by SREBP activation and peroxisome proliferation – rodent-specific, threshold phenomenon.  
3. Human relevance LOW; no signal in epidemiological studies (FDA, 2022).

III.  OVERALL WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE CONCLUSION  

At the maximum tolerated human dose (80 mg/day) administered lifelong:

High-probability effects (≥ 1 %)  
• Mild hepatocellular enzyme elevations (adaptive)  
• Myalgia/myopathy (adverse but reversible)

Medium-probability effects (0.1–1 %)  
• Persistent transaminase > 3×ULN (adverse)  
• New-onset type 2 diabetes (adverse, irreversible)  
• Mild cognitive complaints (adaptive/reversible)

Low-probability effects (< 0.1 %)  
• Rhabdomyolysis with secondary acute kidney injury (adverse)  
• Clinically significant DILI or liver failure (adverse)  
• Cataracts or other ocular pathology (uncertain)  
• Cancer promotion (unlikely, rodent-specific)

All clinically meaningful toxicities are routinely monitorable with standard laboratory tests (ALT/AST, CK, fasting glucose, creatinine) and patient-reported symptoms. Most are reversible upon dose reduction or discontinuation, except for diabetes and severe renal/liver failure if damage becomes established.

Data gaps / uncertainties  
• Long-term (> 30 y) statin exposure data are limited.  
• Inter-individual variability (OATP1B1*5, SLCO1B1 c.521T>C, CYP3A4/5 polymorphisms) can markedly alter risk.  
• Limited human histopathology because biopsies are rare.

Overall toxicodynamic activity is MEDIUM with organ-specific high activity in liver and muscle; systemic availability to other tissues is LOW. The therapeutic benefit–risk balance remains favorable in dyslipidaemic adults, but at the MTD a life-long regimen requires periodic clinical monitoring for hepatic enzymes, CK, renal function, and glycaemic control.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Chemical assessed  
• Name: Prednisone (synonyms: 17-Hydroxy-11-dehydrocorticosterone acetate, Meticorten, Deltasone, CAS 53-03-2)  
• Pharmaceutical class: Synthetic glucocorticoid pro-drug that is rapidly reduced in the liver to prednisolone, the active moiety.  
• Typical human oral bioavailability (prednisolone): 70–100 % (variable but high).  

Toxicological profile requested  
Scenario: An adult human is given a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)* of prednisone by the oral route every day for an entire lifetime.  
*For a glucocorticoid, “MTD” in clinical oncology/auto-immune trials is ≈ 1–2 mg kg-1 day-1 (≈ 70–140 mg day-1 in a 70-kg adult). This is well above the long-term therapeutic range (5-20 mg day-1).  

Weight-of-evidence data sources  
• FDA prescribing information (2018 update, ref 1)  
• EMA assessment report (ref 2)  
• ICH and WHO monographs on systemic corticosteroids  
• Long-term follow-up studies in transplant, oncology or rheumatology patients receiving ≥ 1 mg kg-1 day-1 for ≥ 1 y (refs 3-8)  
• Animal chronic toxicity studies (rat, dog, monkey; refs 9-11)  

Overall toxicological judgement  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent, wide-ranging endocrine modulator).  
Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral absorption, low first-pass loss, long t½ of active metabolite, high Vd).  

====================================================
1. CHRONIC FUNCTIONAL AND STRUCTURAL EFFECTS
====================================================
The table below groups the major organ systems affected, describes functional outcome, supportive histopathology/clinical pathology, mechanism, adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability.

(✓ = well documented in humans; † = best evidence from animals)

A. Endocrine / HPA Axis  
• Effect: Suppression of hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis → secondary adrenal insufficiency (✓).  
• Histopathology: Adrenal cortical atrophy (zona fasciculata/reticularis) seen in long-term dog & rat studies (†).  
• Mechanism: Chronic exogenous glucocorticoid → negative feedback ↓ CRH & ACTH → adrenal trophic deprivation.  
• Adverse vs adaptive: ADVERSE once insufficiency manifests (adrenal crisis possible).  
• Reversibility: Often reversible within months but can take ≥ 1 y; incomplete recovery in a minority.  
• Monitoring: Basal AM cortisol, ACTH stimulation test, clinical signs (fatigue, hypotension).

B. Metabolic  
1. Carbohydrate/Lipid  
   • Hyperglycaemia → steroid-induced diabetes (✓).  
   • Dyslipidaemia (↑ TG, ↑ LDL, ↓ HDL) (✓).  
   • Mechanism: GR-regulated gluconeogenic enzymes (PEPCK, G6Pase); antagonism of insulin signaling; lipolysis.  
   • Adverse, largely reversible after cessation; can persist if β-cell reserve lost.  
   • Monitoring: Fasting glucose, HbA1c, lipid panel.

2. Protein  
   • Muscle wasting, proximal myopathy, negative nitrogen balance (✓).  
   • Histology: Type II fiber atrophy; decreased protein synthesis.  
   • Reversibility: Partial; improves with withdrawal + physiotherapy.  
   • Monitoring: CK levels, muscle strength testing.

C. Skeletal  
• Osteoporosis, vertebral & rib fractures (✓).  
• Histopathology: ↓ trabecular bone, thinning cortices; high-dose rat/dog studies show 20–40 % BMD loss (†).  
• Mechanism: GR-mediated ↓ osteoblastogenesis & ↑ osteoclast survival; ↓ intestinal Ca absorption; ↑ renal Ca loss; ↓ sex steroids.  
• Adverse, partly irreversible; some recovery with bisphosphonates/calcitriol.  
• Monitoring: DEXA every 12–24 mo, vertebral imaging, serum bone turnover markers.

D. Immune System / Infection  
• Effect: Broad immunosuppression → ↑ frequency & severity of bacterial, viral, fungal infections; poor vaccine response (✓).  
• Histopathology: Lymphoid tissue involution (thymus, spleen, lymph nodes) (†).  
• Mechanism: GR-induced apoptosis of lymphocytes, suppression of NF-κB & AP-1 cytokine transcription.  
• Adverse; reversible within weeks for circulating lymphocytes, months for lymphoid organs.  
• Monitoring: CBC with differential, CRP, vigilant infection screening.

E. Cardiovascular / Renal  
• Hypertension & fluid retention (✓) – mineralocorticoid cross-reactivity (at MTD).  
• Hypokalaemia (✓) – renal K⁺ wasting.  
• Accelerated atherosclerosis (indirect via lipids, glucose).  
• Adverse, reversible for BP/electrolytes, not for atherosclerotic plaque.  
• Monitoring: BP, electrolytes, ECG, echocardiogram.

F. Ocular  
• Posterior sub-capsular cataracts (✓).  
• Open-angle glaucoma (✓).  
• Mechanism: Lens epithelial oxidative stress & protein cross-linking; ↑ outflow resistance in trabecular meshwork.  
• Cataract = irreversible (surgical correction); glaucoma damage irreversible (pressure controllable).  
• Monitoring: Slit-lamp exam, IOP measurement every 6–12 mo.

G. Gastrointestinal / Liver  
• Peptic ulcer & GI bleeding (risk ↑ with NSAIDs) (✓).  
• Steatosis and glycogen hepatopathy (rare) (✓).  
• Mechanism: ↓ prostaglandin synthesis, impaired mucosal repair; enhanced glycogen synthesis.  
• Mostly reversible; ulcers heal, hepatopathy resolves with withdrawal.  
• Monitoring: Endoscopy if symptomatic, LFTs.

H. Neuropsychiatric  
• Mood changes, euphoria, irritability, depression, psychosis, cognitive impairment (✓).  
• Mechanism: GR-rich limbic structures, altered monoamine turnover, neurotrophin-down-regulation.  
• Usually reversible within weeks, but severe psychiatric episodes may relapse.  
• Monitoring: Psychiatric assessment, sleep evaluation.

I. Dermatologic  
• Skin atrophy, striae, easy bruising, delayed wound healing (✓).  
• Mechanism: ↓ collagen synthesis & fibroblast activity.  
• Partly reversible; striae permanent.  
• Monitoring: Physical examination.

J. Avascular Necrosis (Osteonecrosis)  
• Common sites: femoral head, humeral head (✓).  
• Mechanism: Glucocorticoid-induced adipocyte hypertrophy & fat embolism in bone microvasculature, apoptosis of osteocytes.  
• Generally irreversible; requires surgical intervention.  
• Monitoring: MRI if early joint pain, X-ray.

K. Growth suppression (only relevant in children/adolescents, but noted).

L. Oncogenicity  
• Epidemiology does not show intrinsic carcinogenicity, but infection-related cancers may rise via immunosuppression (Kaposi sarcoma, lymphomas) (ref 12).  

====================================================
2. MECHANISTIC OVERVIEW
====================================================
Prednisone → hepatic 11-β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase-1 → prednisolone (active).  
Prednisolone binds cytosolic glucocorticoid receptor (GR α). The ligand-GR complex translocates to the nucleus to:  
a) Transactivate GRE-containing anti-inflammatory genes (e.g., annexin-1, IkB).  
b) Transrepress NF-κB/AP-1 driven genes (IL-1, IL-6, TNFα).  
Widespread gene modulation in > 20 % of human transcriptome explains pleiotropic toxicity:
• Catabolic state (muscle, bone, skin) – up-regulation of ubiquitin-ligases (MuRF-1, Atrogin-1), down-regulation of collagen I/III, osteocalcin.  
• Metabolic shift – increased gluconeogenesis enzymes, decreased GLUT4 translocation.  
• Mineralocorticoid cross-talk – at high serum levels prednisolone occupies MR in kidney.  
• Apoptosis of T-cells and B-cells via BIM and suppression of BCL-2.  
• Angiogenesis inhibition & endothelial dysfunction contribute to avascular necrosis.

====================================================
3. CLASSIFICATION OF EACH EFFECT
====================================================
• Adverse vs Adaptive: Most chronic outcomes at MTD are adverse. A few early biochemical changes (transient leukocytosis, hepatic glycogen accumulation) are adaptive.  
• Reversibility:  
   – Rapidly reversible (< weeks): leukocytosis, BP/hypokalaemia, hyperglycaemia (if no diabetes), liver glycogen.  
   – Slowly reversible (months): HPA suppression, myopathy, skin atrophy.  
   – Irreversible or poorly reversible: cataracts, glaucoma optic nerve damage, vertebral fractures, avascular necrosis, severe osteoporosis if fracture occurred, striae.  
• Clinical monitoring options summarised above.

====================================================
4. UNCERTAINTIES & DATA GAPS
====================================================
• Lifelong human data at true MTD are limited; most derive from high-dose therapy lasting 1–5 years. Extrapolation assumes effects plateau rather than continuously worsen.  
• Genetic polymorphisms (GR NR3C1 variants, 11β-HSD1 activity) modify individual susceptibility.  
• Concomitant medications (e.g., azoles, rifampin) alter systemic exposure; ionic effects of water balance may differ.  
• Animal studies show hepatic tumours in some strains after huge doses, but relevance to humans < 1 %.  

====================================================
CONCLUSION
====================================================
Chronic daily administration of an MTD of prednisone is predicted to produce a high overall toxicological burden characterised by:  
• High toxicodynamic activity (broad GR-mediated genomic effects) with high systemic availability.  
• Multi-system adverse effects, many monitorable and partly reversible, but a subset (osteonecrosis, cataract, established osteoporosis with fractures, ocular nerve damage) are largely irreversible.  
• Mechanistic basis is well understood: sustained activation of glucocorticoid receptor pathways producing catabolism, endocrine feedback suppression, immunosuppression and mineralocorticoid mimicry.  
Regular clinical surveillance (bone densitometry, metabolic panels, BP, ocular exams, adrenal testing) and prophylactic co-therapies (Ca/vit-D, bisphosphonates, PPI, infection prophylaxis) are essential to mitigate, but not eliminate, chronic toxicity at the MTD.

Adrenal suppression and HPA-axis recovery after lifelong prednisone exposure

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Based on the studies and expert reviews contained in the supplied resources, lifetime daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, CAS-No. 1746-01-6) would be expected to produce the following chronic toxicological profile in an adult human.

1. Liver effects
   • Functional / pathology findings – hepatocyte necrosis and fatty metamorphosis were reported in rats and mice given TCDD (Schwetz et al., 1973; King et al., 1973).  Chronic dietary exposure produced liver tumours in multiple rodent studies (Van Miller et al., 1977; IARC, 1997) and is associated with increased cancer mortality in highly exposed human cohorts (Seveso zone data, cited in Dr. Masten’s review).
   • Mechanism – high-affinity binding of TCDD to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) in hepatocytes triggers transcriptional activation of genes that regulate xenobiotic metabolism (e.g. AHH/CYP1A) and cell growth.  Sustained AhR signalling leads to oxidative stress, regenerative proliferation and promotion of initiated cells, which together explain liver necrosis, enzyme induction and hepatocarcinogenesis (Resources: “AhR-mediated mechanism of dioxin toxicity”; Table 9 AHH induction; tumour-promotion discussion).
   • Adverse/adaptive – Enzyme induction alone can be adaptive at low dose, but at the MTD the necrosis and tumour formation are clearly adverse.
   • Reversibility – Necrosis/fatty change may regress if exposure stops early; neoplastic changes are irreversible.
   • Monitorability – Serum transaminases, γ-glutamyltransferase, bilirubin, and imaging for steatosis or masses are standard clinical tools.
   • Toxicodynamic activity: High   |   Systemic availability: High (potent, orally bioavailable, long half-life).

2. Thymic and immune effects
   • Findings – The thymus is identified as a principal target organ in several species (International Agency for Research on Cancer, 1977).  Rodent studies have consistently shown thymic involution and immune suppression after chronic exposure; human epidemiology reviews note altered immune markers in the most highly exposed cohorts (Dr. Masten summary).
   • Mechanism – AhR-mediated alteration of cytokine and growth-factor gene expression disrupts T-cell maturation, leading to thymic atrophy and depressed humoral and cellular responses.
   • Adverse/adaptive – Immunosuppression is adverse.
   • Reversibility – Partial recovery of thymic mass and function may occur after cessation, but prolonged high-dose exposure may cause lasting immune deficits.
   • Monitorability – Peripheral lymphocyte subsets, serum immunoglobulins, delayed-type hypersensitivity testing.
   • Toxicodynamic activity: High   |   Systemic availability: High.

3. Dermatological effect – Chloracne
   • Findings – Severe chloracne was reported in >90 % of workers handling chlorinated dioxins (TCAOB case-series; analogous to TCDD).  Chloracne is the hallmark cutaneous effect of dioxin-like chemicals.
   • Mechanism – AhR activation in sebocytes/keratinocytes alters differentiation, causing hyperkeratinisation and cyst formation.
   • Adverse/adaptive – Adverse (disfiguring skin disease).
   • Reversibility – Often prolonged; lesions may resolve slowly after exposure ends.
   • Monitorability – Clinical dermatologic examination and photography.
   • Toxicodynamic activity: High (skin target)   |   Systemic availability: High.

4. Endocrine and metabolic alterations
   • Findings – TCDD induces hepatic microsomal enzymes involved in steroid metabolism (Resource: hepatic effects section citing microsomal induction).  Epidemiologic summaries show associations with altered thyroid and steroid endpoints.
   • Mechanism – AhR-driven CYP induction accelerates metabolism of endogenous hormones, leading to systemic hormonal imbalance.
   • Adverse/adaptive – Largely adaptive at low levels; at the MTD sustained endocrine disruption is adverse.
   • Reversibility – Likely reversible when exposure ceases, provided no secondary pathology has developed.
   • Monitorability – Serum T4/T3, cortisol, sex hormones.
   • Toxicodynamic activity: Medium   |   Systemic availability: High.

5. Carcinogenicity (systemic)
   • Evidence – IARC classifies TCDD as Group 1 (known human carcinogen).  Increased all-cancer and site-specific mortality were found in the highest human exposure cohorts; lifetime animal bioassays showed multisite tumours (liver, thyroid, etc.).
   • Mechanism – Persistent AhR activation acts as a tumour promoter; receptor binding is necessary but not sufficient—additional genomic changes accrue over time.
   • Adverse/adaptive – Adverse, irreversible.
   • Monitorability – Routine cancer screening; no specific biomarker beyond serum TCDD itself.
   • Toxicodynamic activity: High   |   Systemic availability: High.

6. Other possible findings at the MTD (based on rodent chronic studies of TCDD or closely related dioxins)
   • Kidney: toxic nephropathy and calculus formation (Tables of non-neoplastic lesions).*  
   • Gastro-intestinal tract: focal inflammation.
   • Cardiovascular: inflammatory/fibrotic changes in myocardium.*
   • Mechanisms for these less-studied effects are presumed secondary to systemic inflammation and oxidative stress driven by AhR signalling.
   • These changes are adverse; partial reversibility is possible except for fibrosis.

*Effects marked with an asterisk were recorded in chronic rodent studies that used TCDD or structurally related dioxins and therefore represent plausible, but less certain, human hazards.

Uncertainties and data gaps
   • No controlled human lifetime oral study exists; extrapolation from high-exposure incidents (e.g., Seveso) and animal MTD studies is required.
   • Species differences in toxicokinetics (half-life, distribution) may influence target-organ dosimetry.
   • Reversibility of immune and endocrine changes in humans at the MTD is not well documented.

Weight-of-evidence conclusion
   Combining rodent chronic bioassays, mechanistic AhR data, and human epidemiology leads to the judgment that lifetime daily ingestion of an MTD of TCDD would result in high systemic availability and high toxicodynamic activity, with a spectrum of adverse effects dominated by liver injury/cancer, immune suppression, chloracne, endocrine disruption, and multisite carcinogenesis.  Adaptive enzyme induction is overshadowed by these adverse outcomes at the MTD.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Summary of chronic oral benzene toxicity at or near a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD)
Chemical: Benzene (CAS 71-43-2)
Principal long-term gavage data set: NTP 103-week study (rats: 0, 50, 100, 200 mg/kg day; mice: 0, 25, 50, 100 mg/kg day; 5 days week-1) together with supporting mechanistic literature cited below.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Absorption: Rapid and extensive after oral dosing (>90 %) (Sabourin et al. 1989).  
• Distribution: Lipophilic parent distributes to fatty tissues; reactive metabolites preferentially reach bone-marrow (Sanders et al. 2001).  
• Metabolism: CYP2E1-mediated oxidation → benzene oxide → phenol → hydroquinone/catechol → 1,4-benzoquinone + ring-opened muconaldehydes (Ross 2000).  
• Excretion: Predominantly urinary conjugates (phenyl- and hydroquinone-glucuronides, muconic acid).  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH – efficient oral absorption and metabolite delivery to target organs.

2. Major functional and pathological findings at the MTD
2.1 Haematopoietic system (critical target)
   a) Pancytopenia characterised by leukopenia, lymphocytopenia, thrombocytopenia and anaemia (LOAEL 25 mg/kg in mice & female rats; NTP 2005 tables 6 & B1).  
   b) Bone-marrow hypocellularity followed by compensatory cell proliferation; increased micronuclei and chromosomal aberrations (Choy et al. 1985; Ciranni et al. 1991).  
   c) Neoplasia – excess malignant lymphoma in male mice at 200 mg/kg and epidemiologically acute myeloid/ non-lymphocytic leukaemia in workers (Rinsky et al. 1981; NTP).  
   d) Lymphoid organ atrophy (thymus, mandibular / mediastinal / mesenteric nodes) at ≥100 mg/kg (NTP).  
Mechanism: Reactive metabolites (HQ, BQ, muconaldehyde) produce oxidative DNA damage, TOP2 inhibition and p53 pathway dysregulation in haematopoietic stem/progenitor cells, leading to apoptosis, clonal expansion and leukaemogenesis (Smith 1996; Yoon et al. 2003).  
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.  
Adversity: Adverse; marrow suppression causes clinical disease.  
Reversibility: Partial for cytopenias if exposure stops early; irreversible for clonal leukaemia.  
Clinical monitoring: Complete blood counts, differential WBC, reticulocytes; bone-marrow biopsy; biomarkers of exposure (urinary t,t-muconic acid, S-phenyl-mercapturic acid).

2.2 Immune function
   • Functional immunosuppression secondary to lymphoid depletion (adaptive immunity ↓, infection risk ↑).  
   • Mechanistic link: same stem-cell toxicity; additional oxidative stress in thymic cortex.  
Adverse, potentially reversible with recovery of marrow; monitorable via lymphocyte subsets and immunoglobulin titres.

2.3 Liver
   • Mild centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy and ↑ liver weight at ≥100 mg/kg in rodents; no severe necrosis (supporting ethylbenzene data not used for hazard call).  
   • Mechanism: Adaptive enzyme induction to metabolise benzene (↑CYP2E1).  
Adaptive, reversible, monitorable via ALT/AST & ultrasound.

2.4 Skin & pigmentation (mice)
   • Increased dermal melanin deposition in all dosed males and 100 mg/kg females (NTP).  
   • Likely secondary to bone-marrow–derived macrophage activation; of low clinical significance.  
Adaptive/neutral, reversible, monitorable by inspection.

2.5 Body-weight / general toxicity
   • Slight body-weight depression (~10 %) at ≥100 mg/kg: adaptive.  
   • Defines MTD along with persistent anaemia (NTP peer-review notes).

3. Overall chronic human health effects expected at a lifetime daily MTD
High systemic availability + high toxicodynamic potency →
   • Early-onset progressive marrow depression → chronic aplastic anaemia, pancytopenia.
   • Elevated lifetime risk of haematological malignancies (AML, MDS, lymphoma).
   • Immunosuppression with infection susceptibility.
Secondary, largely adaptive effects: hepatic enzyme induction, mild hepatomegaly, skin pigment changes.

4. Risk characterisation & clinical management
   • Adverse effects dominate; NO known safe life-time MTD for humans.  
   • Continuous clinical surveillance essential: full blood counts every 1–3 months, liver function annually, urinary benzene metabolites for compliance, haematology consult for marrow biopsy on abnormalities.

5. Data gaps / uncertainties
   • Human oral-exposure data limited; extrapolation from gavage and inhalation introduces uncertainty.  
   • Individual variability in CYP2E1 & NQO1 polymorphisms may alter susceptibility.  
   • Threshold for leukaemogenesis not clearly defined; linear low-dose extrapolation currently used by US-EPA.

Conclusion
Daily lifetime administration of a benzene MTD would produce high, systemic, adverse and partly irreversible haematological toxicity with a high probability of leukaemia. Other organ changes are mostly adaptive and reversible. The toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability are both classified as HIGH.

Key citations from resources: Sabourin et al. 1989; Sanders et al. 2001; NTP Benzene GMM 8 report (Tables 6, A1–A4); Ross 2000; Smith 1996; Yoon 2003; Rinsky 1981.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Chemical evaluated  
CAS No.: 15502-74-6  
Preferred name: arsenite (most commonly encountered as sodium arsenite, NaAsO2; oxidation state +III)

Overall toxicological profile  
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent multisystem toxicant, established human carcinogen)  
• Systemic availability after oral exposure: HIGH (70–95 % GI absorption; rapid distribution to liver, skin, kidney, lung, bone; extensive systemic re-disposition from methylated metabolites)  

The discussion below reflects a lifetime, daily administration at (or just below) the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) in an adult human. In experimental animals this corresponds roughly to 0.5–1 mg As/kg bw per day; epidemiological evidence shows severe toxicity in human populations consuming 0.05–0.2 mg As/kg bw per day from drinking-water for decades (ATSDR 2007; NRC 2001; EFSA 2009).

1. KINETIC CONSIDERATIONS (ADME)  
• Absorption: Passive diffusion of un-charged H3AsO3 in stomach and proximal intestine; ≥70 % enters portal blood within 4 h.  
• Distribution: Strong affinity for –SH rich tissues; accumulates in skin, hair, nails, liver and kidney; crosses placenta.  
• Metabolism: Step-wise oxidative methylation in liver (ArsM, AS3MT) → monomethylarsonous acid (MMAIII), monomethylarsonic acid (MMAV), dimethyl­arsinous acid (DMAIII), dimethylarsinic acid (DMAV). Tri- and pentavalent methylated species circulate, some more reactive than parent.  
• Excretion: 60–70 % total As in urine within 2–3 days, primarily as DMA and MMA; ~5 % as inorganic AsIII/AsV. Slow release from keratinized tissues extends half-life to weeks.  
Implication – Continuous daily dosing at the MTD produces quasi-steady internal concentrations capable of overwhelming detoxification and repair processes.

2. ORGAN-SPECIFIC TOXICITY, MECHANISM, PATHOLOGY AND CLINICAL RELEVANCE  

2.1 Skin  
Observed / predicted findings  
• Hyperpigmentation (“raindrop” pattern), hypopigmentation, palmar/plantar hyperkeratosis, Bowen’s disease (in-situ SCC), invasive SCC and BCC.  
Mechanism  
• Arsenite binds cysteine residues in keratinocyte proteins, induces ROS and AP-1/NF-κB activation → proliferative signalling; interferes with DNA repair and causes global hypomethylation.  
Adverse?  Yes (lesions & cancers).  
Reversible?  Pigmentation changes partially reversible if exposure ceases early; hyperkeratosis often persistent; malignant lesions irreversible.  
Monitorable?  Dermatological exam, biopsy.

2.2 Hematopoietic system  
Findings  
• Dose-dependent anemia (normo- or micro-cytic), leukopenia, thrombocytopenia; bone-marrow hypocellularity in rodent studies (Elliot et al. 2000; Waalkes et al. 2004).  
Mechanism  
• Direct inhibition of pyruvate dehydrogenase + ATP depletion in rapidly dividing marrow cells; oxidative damage; epigenetic repression of erythroid transcription factors.  
Adverse?  Yes.  
Reversible?  Generally reversible within weeks to months after exposure stops if marrow not fibrosed.  
Monitorable?  Full blood count, reticulocytes.

2.3 Liver  
Findings  
• Persistent elevation of ALT, AST, ALP; fatty change, portal fibrosis; in mice: hepatocellular adenoma/carcinoma at ≥10 ppm As in drinking-water (Waalkes 2007).  
Mechanism  
• Mitochondrial dysfunction, glutathione depletion, β-oxidation inhibition → steatosis; chronic inflammation and oxidative DNA damage promote fibrosis and tumorigenesis.  
Adverse?  Yes.  
Reversible?  Steatosis potentially reversible; fibrosis and tumors irreversible.  
Monitorable?  Serum transaminases, ultrasound, elastography.

2.4 Kidney  
Findings  
• Proximal tubular degeneration, vacuolation; raised serum creatinine/BUN; microalbuminuria in chronically exposed human populations (Tseng 2003).  
Mechanism  
• AsIII accumulation in S3 segment, binding to mitochondrial enzymes, ROS-mediated cell loss; MMAIII particularly nephrotoxic.  
Adverse?  Yes.  
Reversible?  Mild functional changes reversible; chronic interstitial nephritis largely irreversible.  
Monitorable?  eGFR, urinary β2-microglobulin, protein.

2.5 Cardiovascular system  
Findings  
• Hypertension, atherosclerosis acceleration, QT prolongation; epidemiology links to ischemic heart disease and stroke (Chen et al. 2011).  
Mechanism  
• Endothelial NO synthase inhibition, oxidative stress, inflammation, dyslipidaemia; epigenetic silencing of KCNH2 gives QT effects.  
Adverse?  Yes.  
Reversible?  Functional vasomotor changes partly reversible; structural atherosclerosis not.  
Monitorable?  Blood pressure, lipid panel, ECG.

2.6 Peripheral & central nervous system  
Findings  
• Symmetric sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy (paresthesia, numbness), mild cognitive deficits. Rodent studies: axonal degeneration, demyelination.  
Mechanism  
• AsIII-dependent inhibition of pyruvate dehydrogenase and creatine kinase → energy failure in neurons; oxidative stress; interferes with glutamate receptors.  
Adverse?  Yes.  
Reversible?  Partial recovery possible; often persistent if long-standing.  
Monitorable?  Nerve-conduction velocity, clinical neuro exam.

2.7 Endocrine & metabolic  
Findings  
• Impaired glucose tolerance / type-2 diabetes; altered thyroid hormones (↓T3, T4), reproductive hormone alterations.  
Mechanism  
• Arsenite inhibits insulin signal transduction at PDK1-Akt; induces pancreatic β-cell oxidative stress; competes with phosphate in ATP, disrupting hormone synthesis.  
Adverse?  Yes.  
Reversible?  Glucose intolerance sometimes reversible; established diabetes unlikely.  
Monitorable?  Fasting glucose, HbA1c, thyroid panel.

2.8 Immunotoxicity  
Findings  
• Suppressed cell-mediated immunity; ↓delayed-type hypersensitivity in humans; ↓splenic T-cell counts in mice.  
Mechanism  
• Th1/Th2 skewing via NF-κB modulation, oxidative stress.  
Adverse?  Yes; may increase infection risk.  
Reversible?  Partially.  
Monitorable?  Lymphocyte subset counts, vaccine response.

2.9 Reproductive & developmental (relevant for adults planning families)  
Findings  
• Reduced semen quality, menstrual disturbances, spontaneous abortions reported in high-exposure regions.  
Mechanism  
• Oxidative stress in germ cells, endocrine disruption.  
Adverse?  Yes.  
Reversible?  Often reversible several spermatogenic cycles after cessation.  
Monitorable?  Semen analysis, hormonal assays.

2.10 Carcinogenicity (systemic)  
Human evidence (IARC 2012)  
• Sufficient evidence for cancers of skin, lung, urinary bladder. Limited to arsenite but consistent across inorganic As species. Animal evidence aligns (Waalkes et al. 2004).  
Mechanistic key events  
• Genotoxic: Chromosomal aberrations, micronuclei via impaired BER/NER, ROS-mediated DNA breaks rather than direct mutagenicity.  
• Epigenetic: DNA hypomethylation, promoter hypermethylation of tumour suppressors (p16, RASSF1A).  
• Pro-proliferative: sustained activation of MAPK/ERK and PI3K/Akt pathways.  
Adverse?  Yes, irreversible.  
Monitorable?  Periodic imaging (low-dose CT for lung), urinalysis cytology, dermatologic surveillance.

3. CLASSIFICATION OF EFFECTS  

High confidence, adverse, often irreversible:  
• Cutaneous hyperkeratosis and skin cancer  
• Carcinomas of bladder, lung, liver  
• Bone-marrow suppression (with sustained dosing)  
• Peripheral neuropathy (long-standing)  
• Chronic kidney or liver fibrosis  
• Cardiovascular disease / atherosclerosis  

Medium confidence, partially reversible:  
• Elevated liver enzymes, fatty liver  
• Hypertension  
• Mild immunosuppression  
• Glucose intolerance  

Low confidence / adaptive or reversible:  
• Early metallothionein induction, transient oxidative-stress response in liver and kidney (adaptive, reversible, non-adverse unless overwhelmed)  

4. WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE STATEMENT  
An extensive human epidemiological database (Taiwan, Bangladesh, Chile, inner Mongolia) plus concordant animal studies and mechanistic data establish arsenite as a potent, multisystem chronic toxicant and carcinogen at ≤0.1 mg/kg bw/day. The MTD scenario used in this query is therefore expected to elicit virtually the full spectrum of adverse outcomes listed above.

5. DATA GAPS & UNCERTAINTIES  
• Precise human MTD is undefined; inter-individual variability in metabolism (AS3MT polymorphisms) can shift toxicity threshold.  
• Some endpoints (autoimmunity, subtle neuro-behaviour) still under investigation.  
• Co-exposures (selenium, folate status) modulate risk.

6. CLINICAL MONITORING RECOMMENDATIONS FOR A HYPOTHETICAL TRIAL  
• Quarterly total and speciated urinary arsenic (exposure biomarker)  
• CBC, liver panel, renal panel every 3–6 months  
• Dermatologic examination yearly  
• ECG yearly; fasting glucose & HbA1c every 6 months  
• Urine cytology and low-dose chest CT starting at year 10  

Ethical note  
Given the established carcinogenicity and multisystem toxicity, deliberate lifetime administration at an MTD would be unethical in humans.

References (representative)  
ATSDR. 2007. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic.  
IARC Monographs Vol. 100C. 2012. Arsenic and arsenic compounds.  
NRC (US). 2001. Arsenic in Drinking Water: 2001 Update.  
Waalkes MP et al. 2004. JNCI 96: 1202–1204.  
Chen Y. et al. 2011. Circulation 123: 1093–1101.  
Tseng C-H. 2003. Kidney Int 63: 1941–1948.  
EFSA. 2009. EFSA Journal 7: 1351.

Conclusion  
Lifetime daily oral administration of arsenite at the MTD would produce high systemic exposure and lead to multiple adverse, largely irreversible health effects, including skin lesions, bone-marrow suppression, liver and kidney damage, cardiovascular disease, neurological impairment, diabetes and several cancers. These outcomes are mechanistically linked to arsenite’s high affinity for thiol groups, mitochondrial inhibition, oxidative stress generation, interference with DNA repair and epigenetic dysregulation.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Chronic oral administration of methyl-mercury (CAS 16056-34-1) at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) would be expected to produce systemic availability that is effectively complete and to result in cumulative toxicity, with the central nervous system (CNS) as the critical target.  The weight-of-evidence below is taken only from the cited resource excerpts.  

1. Functional (clinical) effects expected in an adult human 
   • Progressive sensory and motor disturbances: spasticity, cerebellar ataxia, exaggerated reflexes, tremor, mental confusion, deafness and blindness (Hilmy 1976; Magas 1985; human outbreaks in Minamata Bay, Japan and Iraq).  
   • Cognitive decline detectable by "sophisticated neuro-psychometric testing" at body burdens previously considered safe (Grandjean 1997).  
   • Weight loss is reported in rodents and is therefore plausible in humans at MTD (Magas 1985).  
   • Possible renal dysfunction; renal damage was seen in rodent studies with methyl- and ethyl-mercury, with ethyl mercury showing the greater severity but indicating that the kidney is a potential target (Magas 1985).  

2. Toxicological histopathology and clinical pathology 
   • Nervous system:  
       – Degeneration of dorsal root ganglia neurons (Magas 1985).  
       – Loss of granule cells in the cerebellar cortex, more pronounced for methyl- than ethyl-mercury (Magas 1985).  
   • Kidney: degenerative changes have been reported in rodents (Magas 1985), although the effect is generally less marked for methyl-mercury than for ethyl-mercury.  
   • Clinical pathology alterations are not well described in the excerpts, but neurological signs (neuro-behavioural testing) and renal function (blood urea nitrogen, creatinine) should be expected.  

3. Mechanistic explanation (why/how the toxicity occurs)  
   • Systemic availability: Oral methyl-mercury is efficiently absorbed and reaches "higher brain levels" than ethyl-mercury (Magas 1985).  
   • CNS toxicity: high brain accumulation leads to selective neuronal degeneration in dorsal root ganglia and cerebellar granule cells, explaining sensory neuropathy and ataxia.  
   • Renal toxicity: mercury complexes accumulate in the kidney, leading to tubular damage (Magas 1985).  

4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
   • CNS effects (sensory loss, ataxia, cognitive impairment): adverse, generally irreversible once established because they are associated with neuronal loss; monitorable by neurological examination, visual-field and audiometric testing.  
   • Renal tubular damage: adverse; partial reversibility may occur at lower exposure once dosing stops, but sustained lifetime MTD exposure would likely lead to chronic loss of function; monitorable by serum creatinine/BUN and urinary biomarkers.  
   • Weight loss: adverse but reversible if exposure ceases; monitorable by body-weight tracking.  

5. Classification of toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability  
   • Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – clear evidence for potent neurotoxicity and kidney involvement at doses close to the MTD (Magas 1985; human outbreaks).  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH – methyl-mercury achieves higher brain levels than ethyl-mercury following oral intake (Magas 1985).  

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
   • Direct long-term controlled human MTD data are not available; conclusions are read across from high-exposure human incidents and animal studies.  
   • Limited quantitative renal pathology information for methyl-mercury relative to ethyl-mercury.  

Weight-of-evidence conclusion: Lifetime, daily oral exposure to the MTD of methyl-mercury in an adult human would lead to severe, cumulative, and largely irreversible CNS toxicity with supportive, though less definitive, evidence for renal injury.  Blood or hair mercury concentrations, together with detailed neuro-behavioural testing, provide practical clinical monitoring tools.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Vinyl chloride (VC; CAS 75-01-4)  

Route & scenario considered: daily oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human for an entire life-span (≈70 years).  
Key data sources: IARC 100F (2012), ATSDR Toxicological Profile (2006), US-EPA IRIS (2000, update in progress), ECHA REACH dossier, NTP Technical Report 167, numerous in-vivo drinking-water/gavage studies in rat/mouse and mechanistic publications on CYP2E1-mediated bioactivation and DNA etheno-adduct formation. Where human oral data are absent, high-quality inhalation epidemiology is used in a read-across manner because the critical steps (rapid absorption, first-pass liver metabolism, formation of identical reactive metabolites) are the same; only the relative delivered dose to the liver differs.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability (ADME) – weight of evidence
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Absorption: ≥90 % of an oral bolus is absorbed in rats; uptake in humans is predicted to be similarly efficient because VC is a small, lipophilic gas (logKow 1.4, MW 62) that readily dissolves in GI fluids.  
Distribution: Partitioning into lipid-rich tissues and particularly the liver (first-pass). Volume of distribution in rodents ≈2 L kg-1.  
Metabolism: Almost entirely by hepatic CYP2E1 → chloroethylene oxide (CEO) ↔ 2-chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). CEO/CAA are electrophilic and can:  
 • form DNA adducts (N7-2-hydroxy-ethyl-gua, 1,N6-etheno-Ade, 3,N4-etheno-Cyt)  
 • alkylate proteins, cause oxidative stress, deplete glutathione.  
Excretion: Reactive metabolites are detoxified via epoxide hydrolase or GSH-conjugation → exhaled CO₂ or urinary thioethers (2-cyano-ethyl-mercapturic acid). Half-life of parent VC in blood is minutes, but DNA adducts persist for weeks.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability = HIGH; toxicodynamic activity = HIGH (potent genotoxicant).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Chronic toxicological profile at the oral MTD
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The MTD is operationally the highest dose that does not shorten life expectancy from non-cancer causes in 2-year bioassays (roughly 50–100 mg kg-bw day-1 in rats/mice). Scaling to humans (~70 kg) gives ≈3–7 g day-1, far above environmental exposures. Predictions below therefore describe a worst-case high-dose situation, not typical environmental risk.

A. Liver – principal target organ  
1. Angiosarcoma of the liver (ASL)  
   • Evidence: unequivocal in humans by inhalation (IARC group 1); identical tumour spectrum in rats/mice after drinking-water or gavage ≥1 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   • Mechanism: CEO/CAA induce etheno-DNA adducts → point mutations in K-ras, p53, erbB. Mutations in endothelial progenitor cells + chronic oxidative stress → malignant vascular proliferation.  
   • Adverse? YES.  
   • Reversibility: NO – once malignant, irreversible.  
   • Clinical monitorability: liver imaging (MRI/CT) and serum tumoral markers (VEGF, AFP) but detection generally late.

2. Hepatocellular carcinoma & cholangiocarcinoma  
   • Same mutagenic MOA as above, plus clonal expansion of hepatocytes under regenerative stimuli.  
   • Adverse, irreversible, monitorable by imaging/biopsy.

3. Non-neoplastic liver lesions  
   a) Micro- and macrovesicular steatosis  
      – Seen in 6-month and 2-year rat studies ≥10 mg kg-1 day-1.  
      – Mechanism: mitochondrial dysfunction & impaired β-oxidation due to protein alkylation; partly adaptive as an early stress response.  
      – Adverse? At high grade yes; low grade mainly adaptive.  
      – Reversible? YES if exposure ceases.  
      – Monitorable: serum ALT/GGT, ultrasound elastography.  
   b) Fibrosis / early cirrhosis, portal hypertension  
      – Activation of stellate cells via ROS / TGF-β pathway.  
      – Adverse, partially reversible at early stages; monitorable (elastography, platelets, imaging).

4. Clinical pathology changes  
   • Persistently elevated ALT, AST, ALP, GGT, bilirubin; prolonged PT; thrombocytopenia secondary to portal hypertension.

B. Bone & connective tissue (the “vinyl-chloride disease”)  
1. Acro-osteolysis (resorption of terminal phalanges)  
   • Human inhalation cohorts; oral rat data limited but related to systemic metabolite levels → read-across justified.  
   • Mechanism: chronic micro-vascular damage + osteoclast activation by aldehyde stress.  
   • Adverse, largely irreversible (bone loss).  
   • Monitorable: hand X-ray, clinical exam.

2. Raynaud-like vasospasm, scleroderma-like dermal thickening  
   • Autoimmune phenomena (ANA positivity up to 30 % of exposed workers).  
   • Partially adaptive early (vasospasm) but progresses to adverse fibrosis.  
   • Reversible: partially for Raynaud’s; dermal fibrosis mostly irreversible.  
   • Clinic: nailfold capillaroscopy, ANA panel.

C. Haematological & immunological  
   • Mild normocytic anaemia, thrombocytopenia, reduced complement seen in workers and in rat 90-day gavage ≥50 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   • Mechanism: direct marrow stromal toxicity + immune complex formation.  
   • Usually reversible after exposure stop; monitorable via CBC, complement C3/C4.

D. Nervous system  
   • CNS: headache, dizziness, slowed reaction time; peripheral: mild sensory neuropathy.  
   • Mechanism: parent VC narcosis + aldehyde adducts on axonal proteins.  
   • At MTD chronic dosing some axonal degeneration observed in rats (sciatic nerve).  
   • Mostly reversible after cessation; monitorable by neuro-conduction studies.

E. Kidney  
   • Mild tubular degeneration, increased urinary β-2-microglobulin in rats at ≥100 mg kg-1 day-1; epidemiology weak.  
   • Mechanism: glutathione conjugates of CAA → cysteine-S-conjugate β-lyase → reactive thioketenes in renal cortex.  
   • Adverse at high doses; partial reversibility; monitorable with serum creatinine, KIM-1, cystatin-C.

F. Reproduction/Development  
   • High-dose rodent oral studies show decreased male fertility and minor skeletal variations. Human data inadequate.  
   • Mechanism: testicular oxidative stress & DNA damage.  
   • Potentially adverse, reversible in males, monitorable via semen analysis.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Overall assessment (expert judgement)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (potent DNA-reactive mutagen with multiple target tissues).  
Systemic availability by oral route: HIGH (rapid absorption, efficient liver delivery).  

Critical chronic hazards at the MTD:  
 • Cancer of the liver (angiosarcoma, hepatocellular carcinoma) – adverse, irreversible, monitorable with imaging but usually detected late.  
 • Chronic progressive liver disease (fibrosis → cirrhosis) – adverse, partially reversible, clinically monitorable.  
 • Bone/vascular/connective tissue changes (“VC disease”) – adverse, largely irreversible, X-ray/clinical monitoring feasible.  
 • Multi-system early changes (steatosis, mild neuropathy, haematological changes) – may be adaptive or adverse depending on severity; mostly reversible and clinically monitorable.

Uncertainties & data gaps:  
 • No direct human oral MTD data; extrapolation from inhalation and animal oral studies introduces kinetic uncertainty.  
 • Genetic polymorphisms in CYP2E1, ALDH2, and GSTT1 can modulate individual susceptibility.  
 • Long-term low-dose non-cancer endpoints (e.g., immunotoxicity) need further clarification.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Conclusion
Lifetime daily oral exposure to the MTD of vinyl chloride would generate a high internal dose of its electrophilic metabolites, leading to sustained genotoxic stress. The weight of evidence predicts severe, primarily hepatic, chronic toxicity with a high probability of multiple cancers, accompanied by extra-hepatic connective-tissue and neurological effects. Most key outcomes are unequivocally adverse and largely irreversible once established, although many early biochemical or functional changes are clinically detectable and, if exposure stops early, partially reversible.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted chronic toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for a lifetime where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that is observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 6-PPQ = 6-PPD-quinone = N-(1,3-dimethyl-butyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine-quinone  
CAS No.: 2754428-18-5

NOTE ON DATA AVAILABILITY  
• No guideline repeat-dose studies on 6-PPQ in mammals are yet publicly available (searches: PubChem, TOXNET, EPA ChemView, ECHA 05/2024).  
• Salmonid acute lethality is well documented, illustrating high reactivity toward biological thiols (Tian et al., 2021, Science 371:185-189).  
• For chronic human hazard prediction the assessment therefore relies on  
  – Read-across from the parent antioxidant 6-PPD (CAS 793-24-8), p-phenylenediamine (PPD) derivatives, and para-benzoquinones (e.g., menadione, p-BQ).  
  – In silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA).  
  – General mechanistic knowledge on redox-active quinones.  
Uncertainties linked to that approach are highlighted.

1. PREDICTED SYSTEMIC AVAILABILITY (ADME)

Physical-chemical parameters (EPI Suite/QSAR estimates)  
MW ≈ 376 g mol⁻¹; logKow 4.7–5.2; water solubility 0.3–1 mg L⁻¹; pKa (phenolic) ≈ 6.9.  

Absorption  
• Oral uptake in humans is expected to be moderate (30–60 %) because of high lipophilicity but MW < 500 g/mol and the presence of a few H-bond acceptors. Quinone electrophilicity may promote glutathione conjugation already in intestinal mucosa.  

Distribution  
• High plasma protein binding (>95 %) predicted due to aromatic/hydrophobic surface.  
• Likely partitioning to lipid-rich tissues (liver, spleen, adrenals).  

Metabolism  
• Phase II conjugation overwhelmingly dominant: Michael addition of GSH, cysteine, N-acetyl-cysteine → mercapturic acids; minor glucuronide/sulfate pathways.  
• Possible one-electron redox cycling to semiquinone radicals → ROS.  

Excretion  
• Conjugates cleared in bile and urine.  

Overall systemic availability classification: MEDIUM (absorption not complete, but sustained daily dosing ensures internal exposure; conjugates retain some reactivity in kidney).

2. TOXICODYNAMIC ACTIVITY

Alerts/experimental analogues  
• Category: electrophilic para-benzoquinones (protein-reactive, GSH depletors, methemoglobin formers, skin sensitizers).  
• OECD Toolbox: protein binding alerts: Michael acceptor, quinone-imide.  
• Parent compound 6-PPD is non-genotoxic in standard assays, but oxidation to the quinone greatly increases electrophilicity; several in silico tools flag 6-PPQ as potential mutagen/clastogen.  

Toxicodynamic potency classification: HIGH (potent electrophile, redox cycling).

3. EXPECTED CHRONIC EFFECTS AT A MAXIMUM TOLERATED DOSE (MTD)

Assumption: daily oral dose that produces minimal (<10 %) body-weight suppression but no overt lethality in sub-chronic rodent studies (~ analogous to 150 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ in rats for 6-PPD; scaled to human ≈15 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹). Effects are summarized per organ system.

3.1 Liver

Observed / predicted findings  
• Clinical chemistry: ↑ALT, ↑AST, ↑ALP, ↑total bilirubin (dose-dependent).  
• Histopathology: centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy (adaptive, CYP induction) progressing to focal necrosis/inflammation at chronic MTD. Lipofuscin accumulation due to oxidative stress; bile-duct proliferation possible.  
Mechanism  
• Quinone–protein adduct formation → mitochondrial dysfunction & oxidative stress; partial depletion of hepatic GSH pool.  
Classification  
– Early enzyme induction: adaptive, reversible, monitorable (serum enzymes).  
– Necrosis / fibrosis: adverse; partially reversible via hepatocyte regeneration if dosing stops; monitorable (transaminases, imaging, biopsy).

3.2 Kidney

Findings  
• Clinical pathology: mild proteinuria, ↑blood urea nitrogen, ↑creatinine.  
• Histology: proximal tubular epithelial degeneration, granular casts; basophilic tubule regeneration; pigment deposition of hemoglobin breakdown products where hemolysis occurs.  
Mechanism  
• Renal excretion of thiol-conjugates → covalent binding in S3 segments; local ROS.  
Classification  
Adverse; partially reversible; monitorable by serum creatinine, eGFR, urinary NAG/KIM-1.

3.3 Hematologic system

Findings  
• Methemoglobinemia (5–15 % MetHb at trough, occasional cyanosis).  
• Heinz-body hemolytic anemia: ↓RBC, ↓Hb, ↑reticulocytes, ↑LDH; spleen enlargement with erythrophagocytosis.  
Mechanism  
• Oxidation of ferrous hemoglobin to ferric state by quinone/semiquinone; direct RBC membrane protein adduction.  
Classification  
Adverse; largely reversible once exposure ceases (RBC T½ 120 d); monitorable by MetHb assay, CBC, haptoglobin.

3.4 Immune & skin

Findings  
• Systemic delayed-type hypersensitivity (cross-reactivity with p-phenylenediamine). Oral dosing seldom provokes overt dermatitis, but patch tests may turn positive.  
Mechanism  
• Haptenation of skin/liver proteins → T-cell mediated response.  
Classification  
Adverse in sensitised individuals; reversible only with avoidance; monitorable via patch testing/IgE (though T-cell mediated).

3.5 Nervous system

Findings (high uncertainty)  
• Subtle peripheral neuropathy (↓NCV), occasional tremor.  
Mechanism  
• Quinone-induced oxidative damage to Schwann-cell proteins analogous to menadione.  
Classification  
Adverse; reversibility uncertain; monitorable via EMG.

3.6 Endocrine & reproduction

Current data on 6-PPD show no reproductive toxicity up to 200 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ in rats; no specific alerts for estrogen/androgen receptor binding. Quinone form unlikely to be potent endocrine disruptor. Chronic MTD exposure therefore expected to be LOW concern for endocrine endpoints.

3.7 Carcinogenicity (lifetime)

Evidence  
• No animal bioassay data for 6-PPQ.  
• p-Benzoquinone inhalation induces nasal tumors in rats/mice (NTP TR-090).  
• Electrophilic quinones generate 8-oxodG, DNA adducts, chromosomal aberrations in vitro.  
Weight-of-evidence: borderline genotoxic, non-threshold MOA plausible.  
Prediction  
• Increased risk for liver and nasal cavity tumors in rodents at high chronic doses; corresponding human lifetime oral MTD likely poses MODERATE carcinogenic risk.  
Classification  
Adverse, irreversible; monitorable only indirectly (α-FP, imaging); very high uncertainty.

3.8 Clinical symptom profile in humans at lifetime MTD

Adaptive phenomena  
• Dark urine (metabolite excretion)  
• Mild serum transaminase elevation  

Adverse manifestations  
• Intermittent fatigue & dyspnoea (MetHb 10 %)  
• Episodes of jaundice (hepatic injury)  
• Mild renal impairment (eGFR 60–80 mL min⁻¹)  
• Possible skin eruptions in sensitised subjects  

4. REVERSIBILITY & MONITORABILITY SUMMARY

Effect → Adaptive/Adverse – Reversible? – Clinical monitoring tool  
• Hepatic enzyme induction → Adaptive – yes – ALT/AST  
• Hepatocellular necrosis/fibrosis → Adverse – partial – LFT, ultrasound, FibroScan  
• Tubular nephropathy → Adverse – partial – serum creatinine, cystatin-C, urinary KIM-1  
• Methemoglobinemia → Adverse – yes – CO-oximetry, CBC  
• Hemolytic anemia → Adverse – yes – CBC, reticulocytes, haptoglobin  
• Skin/immune sensitization → Adverse – no – patch test  
• Carcinogenicity → Adverse – no – imaging, tumor markers (limited predictivity)

5. WEIGHT-OF-EVIDENCE RATING

Systemic availability: MEDIUM  
Toxicodynamic reactivity: HIGH  
Overall chronic hazard at lifetime oral MTD: MODERATE to HIGH (driven by redox cycling, protein binding, potential genotoxicity).  

6. KEY UNCERTAINTIES / DATA GAPS

• No repeat-dose rodent study on 6-PPQ – magnitude of hepatotoxic and nephrotoxic doses extrapolated from parent 6-PPD and benzoquinone analogues.  
• Genotoxic potential in vivo not yet tested (Ames for 6-PPQ reportedly negative but not OECD-compliant; no micronucleus data).  
• Human kinetic data lacking; assumption of similar metabolism to rodents.  
• Potential metabolites unique to 6-PPQ (e.g., semiquinone radicals) not fully characterised.

7. CONCLUSIONS

At continuous lifetime administration of a maximum tolerated oral dose, 6-PPQ is expected to induce:

1. Oxidative liver and kidney injury (primary target organs).  
2. Hematological effects (methemoglobinemia, hemolytic anemia).  
3. Immunologic sensitisation.  
4. Possible peripheral neurotoxicity at high exposures.  
5. A plausible, though unconfirmed, carcinogenic risk driven by electrophilic DNA interactions.

Most functional changes can be detected and managed clinically, and some (enzyme induction, methemoglobinemia, early tubular changes) are reversible once exposure ceases. However, organ fibrosis, hypersensitisation, and tumorigenesis are irreversible adverse outcomes.

Further guideline-conform 90-day, chronic and genotoxicity studies are strongly recommended to refine the risk assessment.

clinical monitoring biomarkers for 6PPQ exposure
structure–activity relationships of PPD-type antidegradants

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Chronic Toxicological Profile of Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8) – Lifetime Daily Oral Administration at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Introductory framing
   • Phenanthrene is a 3-ring polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH), log Kow ≈ 4.5–4.6, water solubility ≈ 1–2 mg L⁻¹ at 25 °C, vapor pressure 6–7 × 10⁻⁴ Pa.  
   • IARC: Group 3 (“not classifiable”; IARC, 2010).  
   • No guideline-quality lifetime oral studies exist in humans; the evaluation below integrates:  
     – Repeated-dose rodent studies (e.g., NTP, 1983; NIH-78-832; 13-wk gavage; 2-yr dermal mouse studies).  
     – Mechanistic work on phenanthrene metabolites (diol-epoxides, quinones).  
     – Read-across from structurally similar low-molecular-weight PAHs (anthracene, pyrene) and the PAH mode-of-action literature.  
   • “MTD” is interpreted as the highest dose that produces minimal overt toxicity but does not reduce lifespan from non-treatment related causes. For phenanthrene this is roughly 50–200 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ in rats/mice; human equivalent ≈ 10–30 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (allometric conversion).  

2. Systemic availability (ADME)
   • Absorption: 20–40 % oral bioavailability in rats; enhanced by dietary fat.  
   • Distribution: highly lipophilic; accumulates in liver, adipose, kidney, brain; Vd > 5 L kg⁻¹.  
   • Metabolism: Phase I oxidation via AhR-inducible CYP1A1/1B1 → phenanthrene 1,2-epoxide → trans-dihydrodiols → diol-epoxides and phenanthrene-quinones. Phase II conjugation (UDP-glucuronyl, sulfotransferase, GST).  
   • Excretion: bile > urine; t½ in rodents ≈ 10–18 h.  
   Overall systemic availability: Medium.  

3. Integrated weight-of-evidence toxicodynamic activity
   • Potency of reactive metabolites (DNA adducts, ROS generation) is lower than high-molecular-weight PAHs (e.g., benzo[a]pyrene) but measurable in vitro and in vivo.  
   • Enzyme induction via AhR is moderate; LOEL ≈ 25 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ in 28-d rat studies.  
   Toxicodynamic activity: Medium.  

4. Observed or predicted chronic effects at lifetime MTD

4.1 Liver
   Functional / clinical observations
     – ↑ liver weight (15–40 %), mild hepatomegaly, occasionally elevated serum ALT/AST (≤ 2-fold).  
     – No persistent cholestasis or marked synthetic dysfunction in animal studies.
   Histopathology
     – Centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy; minimal single-cell necrosis; occasional pigment deposition.  
   Mechanism
     – AhR activation → CYP1A induction → increased smooth ER → adaptive hypertrophy; reactive metabolites generate low-level oxidative stress causing sporadic cell death.  
   Adversity classification / reversibility
     – Hypertrophy: adaptive, reversible within weeks after cessation; monitorable (liver enzymes, ultrasound).  
     – Single-cell necrosis: mild, adverse if sustained; reversible through regeneration at MTD.  

4.2 Kidney
   Functional / clinical pathology
     – Small, dose-related ↑ BUN/creatinine rarely exceeds 1.5-fold; urinary β-2-microglobulin occasionally elevated.  
   Histopathology
     – Proximal tubular epithelial degeneration/regeneration foci; hyaline casts in outer medulla.  
   Mechanism
     – Local accumulation of phenanthrene-quinones → redox cycling → ROS → tubular oxidative damage.  
   Adversity / reversibility
     – Adverse if progressive; generally reversible at MTD after removal; monitorable (serum creatinine, eGFR, urinary proteins).  

4.3 Hematological system / bone marrow
   Observations
     – Mild normocytic anemia (Hgb ↓ 5–10 %), occasional reticulocytosis; white cell counts largely unaffected.  
   Mechanism
     – Systemic oxidative burden + PAH-DNA adducts in marrow precursors suppress erythropoiesis.  
   Adversity / reversibility
     – Adverse but mild; reversible; monitorable via CBC.  

4.4 Endocrine axis (thyroid and steroid homeostasis)
   Observations
     – ↓ total serum T4 (~20 %), TSH slightly ↑; free T4 normal.  
   Mechanism
     – CYP1A-mediated induction of thyroid hormone-conjugating UGTs → ↑ T4 clearance; classic adaptive PAH response.  
   Adversity / reversibility
     – Generally considered adaptive/compensatory; reversible; monitorable (thyroid panel).  

4.5 Nervous system
   Observations
     – No significant neurobehavioral deficits in rodent 90-d studies; but phenanthrene crosses the BBB.  
     – Some in vitro data show inhibition of gap junctional intercellular communication, indicating potential subtle neurotoxicity.  
   Mechanism
     – Lipid peroxidation and ROS in neural tissue; no definitive in vivo confirmation.  
   Adversity
     – Uncertain (data gap); if present likely low-grade and reversible; difficult but potentially monitorable with neuropsychological testing.  

4.6 Immunotoxicity
   Observations
     – Moderate suppression of T-dependent antibody response (≥ 100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ in mice).  
   Mechanism
     – AhR-mediated modulation of cytokine networks; generation of reactive metabolites affecting lymphocyte DNA.  
   Adversity
     – Adverse; partially reversible; monitorable (vaccine challenge-derived antibody titres, lymphocyte phenotyping).  

4.7 Mutagenicity / Carcinogenicity
   Evidence summary
     – Phenanthrene is mutagenic in Salmonella TA100 ± S9 and induces micronuclei in cultured human lymphocytes via diol-epoxides.  
     – 2-yr dermal mouse studies: increased incidence of skin papillomas at high doses; no strong systemic tumor signal.  
     – Inadequate oral carcinogenicity data; lifetime systemic dose modelling suggests low but non-zero genotoxic cancer risk.  
   Mechanistic basis
     – Formation of bay-region diol-epoxides → covalent DNA adducts → miscoding lesions; quinone redox cycling → oxidative DNA damage.  
   Adversity
     – Adverse; irreversible once mutation fixed; not clinically monitorable until tumour forms; cancer risk is the critical effect driving human health protective limits.  

5. Summary table

Effect   | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Liver hypertrophy | Adaptive | Yes | Yes (enzymes, imaging)
Hepatocellular necrosis | Adverse (mild) | Yes | Yes (ALT/AST)
Kidney tubular change | Adverse | Yes | Yes (creatinine, urine proteins)
Mild anemia | Adverse (mild) | Yes | Yes (CBC)
↓ total T4 | Adaptive | Yes | Yes (thyroid panel)
Immune suppression | Adverse | Partial | Yes (antibody response)
Genotoxicity / cancer | Adverse | No | No (pre-clinical); monitor only by surveillance for tumours

6. Key uncertainties and data gaps
   • No guideline 2-yr oral bioassay – carcinogenic potency must be extrapolated from dermal/intraperitoneal data and read-across.  
   • Sparse clinical pathology data in long-term rodent studies.  
   • Human biomonitoring data limited to occupational mixtures, not phenanthrene alone.  
   • Neurotoxicity evidence largely in vitro; in vivo relevance unclear.  

7. Overall conclusion
At a lifetime daily MTD, phenanthrene exhibits moderate systemic availability and medium toxicodynamic activity. Chronic administration is expected to produce primarily adaptive hepatic enzyme induction with accompanying liver enlargement, mild reversible kidney and hematological changes, modest endocrine adjustments, and some degree of immunosuppression. The principal long-term health concern is a low but measurable genotoxic cancer risk driven by formation of diol-epoxide DNA adducts. Most non-cancer effects are reversible and amenable to routine clinical monitoring; carcinogenic risk is irreversible once initiated and must be minimized by limiting exposure rather than post-exposure clinical management.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted chronic toxicological effects (if any) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for a lifetime where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that is observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, TPP; CAS 115-86-6) is an aryl-phosphate flame-retardant/plasticiser that is readily absorbed after oral administration, is extensively metabolised to diphenyl phosphate (DPHP) and phenyl-hydroxy-phenyl phosphates, and is eliminated mainly in urine (ECHA 2021; Ball et al. 2014). The summaries below integrate the publicly available 28-day, 90-day and limited chronic studies in rats and mice, in-vitro mechanistic work, and human biomonitoring information, assuming that an adult human is dosed daily for a lifetime at the experimental “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD).  Because no human MTD exists, the experimental rat MTD (≈ 200–400 mg/kg bw day) was used as a surrogate and scaled allometrically (≈ 70–140 mg/kg bw day for humans).  All conclusions follow a weight-of-evidence approach.

1. Overall toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability  
• Systemic availability: High.  Oral absorption ≥ 70 %, log Kow ≈ 4.6, extensive first-pass metabolism but plasma/organ concentrations are reached (ECHA dossier, kinetic study TK-2017-01).  
• Intrinsic toxicodynamic potential: Medium to High.  Principal modes of action are liver enzyme induction via CAR/PXR, secondary thyroid hormone depletion, weak anti-cholinesterase activity, and nuclear-receptor mediated endocrine modulation.  No strong direct cytotoxicity or DNA reactivity is seen (negative in Ames, in-vitro micronucleus, COMET; NTP report 2015).  

2. Target organs, functional changes, pathology and mechanistic interpretation  

2.1 Liver  
Evidence  
• 28-day rat gavage ≥ 125 mg/kg: ↑ liver weight (≈ +40 %), centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy, minimal bile-duct proliferation; ALT, AST, ALP slightly ↑ (ECHA key study, OECD 407).  
• 90-day rat dietary 0, 80, 400, 800 ppm (♂≈ 4, 21, 40 mg/kg): LOAEL 21 mg/kg (↑ relative liver weight, hypertrophy); NOAEL 4 mg/kg (Dow 2009, unpublished).  

Mechanism  
Activation of CAR/PXR and PPAR α leads to induction of CYP2B/3A, UGTs, SULTs.  The adaptive proliferative signal causes centrilobular hypertrophy; sustained enzyme induction increases oxidative load, occasionally progressing to bile-duct hyperplasia at very high doses.  

Assessment  
• Nature: Primarily adaptive at ≤ two-fold weight increase; adverse when accompanied by cholestasis, necrosis or sustained bile-duct proliferation (> MTD).  
• Reversibility: Yes; hypertrophy resolves within 2–4 weeks post-exposure (recovery arm in 28-day study).  
• Clinical monitoring: Serum transaminases, γ-GT, bilirubin; liver ultrasound or elastography.  

2.2 Thyroid hormone disruption  
Evidence  
• 28-day rat (≥ 50 mg/kg): Total and free T4 ↓ (-25 – -50 %), TSH normal or slightly ↑; thyroid weight minimally ↑, no follicular cell hypertrophy (OECD 407 study).  
• In vitro: TPHP activates human PXR (EC50 ≈ 3 µM) leading to UGT-induction, accelerating T4 glucuronidation (Rosenmai et al. 2017, Toxicol. Sci.).  

Mechanistic link  
Hepatic enzyme induction ↑ clearance of T4 → mild hypothyroxinaemia.  

Assessment  
• Adversity: At MTD, circulating T4 is significantly depressed; if sustained lifelong, could be adverse (subclinical hypothyroidism).  
• Reversibility: High – hormone levels normalise within days after cessation.  
• Monitoring: Serum free/total T4, TSH; clinical evaluation for fatigue, weight change.  

2.3 Endocrine / reproductive system  
Evidence  
• Male rat 90-day (400 mg/kg): ↓ epididymis and prostate weight, slight ↓ plasma testosterone; spermatogenic staging normal.  
• In-vitro: Weak antagonist in AR-CALUX (IC50 > 10-5 M); partial agonist of ER-β, modulator of PPAR-γ (Houck et al. 2019).  

Mechanism  
Binding to steroid receptors and PPARs alters steroidogenesis gene networks; indirect effects via CAR/PXR induction modify sex-steroid metabolism.  

Assessment  
• Adversity: Borderline at MTD; reproductive performance unaffected in one-generation screening study up to 125 mg/kg (NICNAS 2017), suggesting mostly adaptive/compensated.  
• Reversibility: Likely yes, based on recovery data.  
• Monitoring: Serum testosterone/estradiol, LH/FSH; semen analysis.  

2.4 Kidney  
Evidence  
• Male rat high-dose: α2u-globulin hyaline-droplet nephropathy and increased kidney weight; minimal basophilic tubules.  Effect absent in females (ECHA dossier).  

Mechanism  
Male-rat specific protein accumulation; not relevant for humans (US EPA α2u-guidance).  

Assessment  
• Human relevance: Low.  
• Reversibility: Yes in rats.  
• Clinical monitoring in humans unnecessary.  

2.5 Nervous system (cholinesterase and behavioural)  
Evidence  
• TPHP inhibits human plasma and RBC AChE in vitro only at ≥ 100 µM (Moser et al. 2017).  
• 28-day rat 400 mg/kg: Brain and RBC AChE inhibition ≤ 15 % (not statistically significant); no clinical signs of neurotoxicity.  

Mechanism  
Aryl-phosphates require oxidative des-arylation to the phosphate-monoester to potently inhibit AChE; TPHP is a poor substrate for this bio-activation.  

Assessment  
• Adversity: Low; at MTD effect is minimal and subclinical.  
• Reversibility: Rapid (days).  
• Monitoring: RBC/Plasma AChE activity.  

2.6 Haematology / clinical chemistry  
Evidence  
• High-dose 90-day rat: mild normocytic anaemia (↓ Hb ~ 10 %), slight ↓ cholinesterase, ↑ ALP, ALT.  
Likely secondary to liver enzyme induction and/or reduced feed intake.  

Assessment  
• Adversity: Mild, reversible.  
• Monitoring: Routine blood count, clinical chemistry.  

2.7 Carcinogenicity & genotoxicity  
• Genotoxicity: Negative in bacterial reverse mutation, in-vitro micronucleus, and mouse lymphoma assays (ECHA, NTP).  
• No 2-year bioassay available.  Chronic liver enzyme induction may predispose to liver tumours in rodents, but threshold-dependent and not necessarily relevant to humans (similar to phenobarbital, WHO IPCS 2005).  
Weight-of-evidence: Carcinogenic potential low; IARC and US EPA have not classified TPHP for cancer.  

3. Summary of chronic oral effects at the experimental MTD  

Organ/System | Effect (histology / function) | Mechanism | Adverse or Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
Liver | Hepatocellular hypertrophy, ↑ liver weight, ± bile-duct proliferation, mild ↑ ALT/AST | CAR/PXR-mediated enzyme induction, oxidative stress | Mostly adaptive; becomes adverse if cholestasis/necrosis | Yes | Liver enzymes, imaging
Thyroid | ↓ Serum T4, minimal follicular hypertrophy | ↑ Hepatic T4 clearance via UGT | Potentially adverse if sustained | Yes | Serum T4/TSH
Endocrine-Reproductive | ↓ accessory sex-organ weight, ↓ testosterone (high dose) | Nuclear-receptor modulation, altered steroid metabolism | Borderline adverse | Yes | Sex-hormone profile, semen
Kidney (male rat) | α2u-globulin nephropathy | Male-rat specific protein binding | Not relevant to humans | Yes | Not required
Nervous system | Very weak AChE inhibition, no overt neurotoxicity | Poor bio-activation to phosphate-monoester | Adaptive/non-adverse | Yes (days) | RBC/Plasma AChE
Blood | Mild anaemia, ↑ ALP | Secondary to liver effects/feed reduction | Adaptive | Yes | CBC, chemistry

4. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No lifetime (2-year) rodent carcinogenicity study—tumour data absent.  
• Human MTD unknown; interspecies extrapolation introduces uncertainty.  
• Limited data on subtle neuro-behavioural outcomes and immune endpoints.  
• Metabolite DPHP has a similar but slightly lower intrinsic potency; cumulative effects were considered qualitatively but quantitative contribution remains uncertain.  

5. Overall conclusion  
At a daily lifetime oral exposure near the experimental MTD, TPHP is systemically available and produces medium-to-high toxicodynamic activity dominated by liver enzyme induction.  The majority of observed changes (liver hypertrophy, thyroid hormone depression, mild endocrine shifts) are adaptive, threshold-dependent, reversible and monitorable in the clinic.  Truly adverse outcomes appear only when the threshold is exceeded (e.g., cholestasis, marked hypothyroxinaemia).  Genotoxicity is absent and carcinogenic potential is considered low, but definitive lifetime studies are lacking.

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that is observed.
